title: Mobilization Mondays: Chapel Hill (mornings)
location: East Franklin Street & South Elliott Road, Chapel Hill, NC, 27514
description:
Please join Engaged Defenders for Democracy on Mondays from 7:30-9 at the corner of Franklin St & Elliott Rd in Chapel Hill for a weekly, peaceful, nonpartisan protest and call to action. Free parking at the Church of Reconciliation, 110 N Elliott Rd.
Across the political spectrum, Americans are disillusioned by leaders who put power over people. We gather to build community, spark hope, and inspire action.
Bring a friend. Bring a sign. Speak up for immigrant rights, free speech, healthcare, social security, the environment, the Constitution and the rule of law, reproductive freedom, job and funding cuts, and the many, many other issues close to our hearts. Bonus points for signs that encourage others to move beyond honking and help turn awareness into action. We often hand out fliers to stopped cars & pedestrians with information about relevant actions folks can take to support their community and defend democracy. We also have a Signal chat for regular attenders. If you are interested in either of these opportunities, ask around for Wendy.
This will be a POSITIVE and nonviolent demonstration. We are here to protect our democracy. We are standing up for what we believe in. We want to inspire people to join us and grow our movement!
Please bring positive signs supporting the movement and resisting what is happening in our country!
Please no mocking signs, no swastikas
We are excited to see you there and stand together for our country!
See you every Monday afternoon engaging drivers in Chapel Hill!
Please join our Substack: https://substack.com/profile/115484101-engaged-defenders-4-democracy
For more ED4D Mobilize Events: https://www.mobilize.us/engagedefendersfordemocracy/https://www.mobilize.us/engagedefendersfordemocracy/event/881591/

title: 2026 UNC Cleantech Summit
description:
The 12th annual UNC Cleantech Summit will take place at the UNC Friday Conference Center on March 9 – 10, 2026. The two-day event will bring together professionals in business, government, and academia from across the Southeast and around the world for discussion, workshops, mentoring, and networking to foster leadership and growth in the Southeast’s cleantech economy. The Summit convenes more than 1,000 attendees and is the largest event of its kind by a university in the United States. Co-hosted by the UNC Institue for the Environment and the Ackerman Center for Excellence in Sustainability at the Kenan-Flagler Business School.

Register here: https://web.cvent.com/event/dc0e55ed-323b-4ce9-bb78-8699d3e904f3/summary?RefId=UNC%20Website

title: Harriet Jacobs in the Attic
description:
Join us for an immersive audiovisual experience as we illuminate Harriet Jacobs, a fugitive North Carolina enslaved person who hid in a tiny attic garret for seven years. While hiding she planned her escape, fled to the North, and eventually became a key figure in the abolitionist movement.

In celebration of Black History Month, we invite you to explore a to-scale recreation of Harriet’s attic space and listen to readings from her book, Incidents in the Life of a Slave Girl, which was published in 1861.

title: The Road Through “Don’t Ask, Don’t Tell”
description:
The Road Through Don’t Ask, Don’t Tell by photographer, oral historian, and U.S. Marine Corps veteran Theresa Scott. This deeply personal documentary exhibition explores the lived experiences of LGBTQIA+ veterans who served under restrictive military policies.
Through intimate portrait photography paired with first-person oral histories, Scott brings forward stories long silenced by institutional policy and cultural pressure. Drawing on her own service, she offers viewers an unflinching look at the realities faced by service members who were expected to serve with honor while hiding essential parts of who they were.
Exhibition Dates: February 1 – April 26, 2026
Artist Talk & Reception: March 26, 5:30–7:00 p.m.

title: Measuring Neural Health: An Energetic Signature of the Living Brain
description:
Quantifying neural health before irreversible decline occurs remains one of the central challenges in neuroscience and medicine. Across epilepsy, psychiatric illness, neurodevelopmental disorders, and neurodegeneration, we lack a unifying physiological standard that defines what a "healthy brain" truly is - and how it begins to fail.

In this talk, I will introduce the Brain Entropy Index (BEI), a physics-grounded biomarker built on two organizing principles of living neural systems: entropy maximization in spatial energy distributions and tight homeostatic regulation of neural activity. In a healthy resting brain, energy is broadly and efficiently distributed across regions, reflecting high entropy, while remaining remarkably stable and tightly controlled.

BEI operationalizes neural health through five measurable energetic properties: lognormal energy distributions, balanced regional allocation, statistical independence between regions, low variance, and temporal stability. I will present results from multimodal EEG, fMRI, and MEG datasets comprising over 525 participants across neurological, psychiatric, neurodevelopmental, and neurodegenerative conditions. BEI not only differentiates healthy and clinical populations, but scales with disease severity and generalizes across diagnostic categories and imaging modalities.

These findings suggest the existence of a disease-agnostic, modality-invariant energetic signature of neural health. By reframing brain function through the lens of energetic organization, BEI provides a quantitative foundation for early detection, patient stratification, and real-time monitoring of therapeutic response.

title: Learn Neurographic Art - PhD Student Mindfulness
description:
Pratt PhD students are invited to discover the calming of Neurographic Art in a hands-on tutorial designed to quiet the mind and spark creativity. Anna Batsakes  from DUKE LIVE FOR LIFE will teach simple techniques that turn lines and shapes into a meditative, expressive artwork, no art experience needed.
Neurographic Art is a mindful drawing practice using continuous lines, shapes, and color to help reduce stress, and encourage emotional balance. The process is intuitive and relaxing, making it accessible to everyone.

title: Mental Health Seminar: The Healing Power of Presence - Mindfulness for Stress and Life Satisfaction
description: What does it really mean to be present, and why does it matter for both our own well-being and the health of our communities? This seminar explores how mindfulness and presence can support mental and emotional health in everyday life. Through practical examples, guided reflection, and accessible mindfulness practices, participants will learn how cultivating awareness can reduce stress, improve emotional regulation, and increase overall life satisfaction. Beyond individual benefits, the session will examine how presence fosters empathy, stronger relationships, and more thoughtful engagement with others. Participants will leave with simple, usable tools for integrating mindfulness into daily routines and for building healthier, more connected communities through intentional awareness. This seminar is open to everyone, whether you are new to mindfulness or looking to deepen your existing practice.

title: Raising Accountability while Managing a Hybrid Team
description:
For employees of Duke University and DUHS.
Continuing Education Credits (CEUs): 0.4

When supervising a hybrid team of employees, you worry whether or not you are getting as much from their job performance as employees who remain physically on the Duke premises. On the other hand, you and your entire team may be working off site full time. Building a bonded, high functioning, and collaborative team requires a combination of knowledge and skills, and clear communication strategies.

Fortunately, technology has come a long way in allowing your team(s) to tap into a multitude of available resources at Duke. This course will teach you the best ways to motivate yourself and your team to connect, inspire and collaborate with one another.

location: The Joel Fund, 822 S White Street, Wake Forest, 27587
description:

Dates: Mon: 3/9; 3/16; 3/23
Please Note: All classes and workshops are for Military Connected Individuals who are 18 years or older. This particular class is for military-connected women over 18 years or older.
Wool can be used as a medium to paint a landscape or a portrait or be sculpted into a 3-dimensional piece of art! This unique art has been used to illustrate children’s books and in stop-motion animation. Artists have begun to truly appreciate the practicality of using this inexpensive natural resource in ornaments, pin cushions, pillows, hanging art, and much, much more!
In this class, students will learn the techniques to make both a flat, 2-dimensional picture and a 3-dimensional sculpture piece. Students will develop self-expression through manipulation of colors and textures of wool.
We offer scholarships for those who cannot afford the registration fee. Email the Operation ART Program Manager at isabella@thejoelfund.org to receive the scholarship promo code with your name and email address.

title: Matt Kaplan Talk & Booksigning
location: The Frank Porter Graham Student Union, Auditorium
description:
This event features Matt Kaplan, science correspondent for The Economist and author of I Told You So! Scientists Who Were Ridiculed, Exiled and Imprisoned… For Being Right. Kaplan will explore how scientific discovery has often been shaped or hindered by social and political forces. He’ll share stories to illuminate why groundbreaking thinkers from the Victorian to the COVID eras have been dismissed and asks the question, "How can we make the scientific ecosystem better?”

Event Details
📅 March 9, 2026
⏰ 4:30 PM
📍 Frank Porter Graham Student Union
🎓 Counts as a Campus Life Experience (CLE) credit
🖊️ Reception & Book signing to follow

Kaplan will offer insight into:

Why many of science’s most persistent problems are centuries old

How cultural and political dynamics shape scientific progress

What students and researchers can do to build a stronger scientific future

Stories of scientists whose ideas were initially dismissed but ultimately changed the world

This promises to be an engaging and energizing event for our campus community across scientific disciplines.

Broken Beakers and Brilliant Minds: Fixing The Machinery of Science

From the energy crisis and feeding eight billion people to defeating cancer and coping with climate change, humanity faces some rather large challenges today. To tackle them, we need science running like a well-oiled machine. Unfortunately, it has more often resembled a clunky old engine: loud, temperamental and prone to breaking down at the worst possible moment.

It is tempting to blame recent funding cuts for science’s current woes, but focusing only on money rather misses the point. Many of science’s biggest problems have been around for years, sometimes centuries. Scientists have long been criticized, sidelined or attacked for having ideas that were too new, too inconvenient or simply too correct for the people in power at the time. Even truly unscientific behaviors, like character assassination and fraud, are not modern inventions… though they do seem to have enjoyed a recent renaissance.

The good news is that longstanding problems are not the same as unsolvable ones. Join Matt Kaplan, science correspondent at The Economist and author of I Told You So! Scientists Who Were Ridiculed, Exiled and Imprisoned… For Being Right, for a spirited and insightful talk on the history of these endemic issues and what we can do to finally consign them to the scientific scrap heap.

title: Webinar: Prospects for The Iranian Economy After the Islamic Republic
description:
This panel discussion will examine the economic prospects of Iran in a potential post-Islamic Republic transition. Experts will discuss immediate economic stabilization challenges, medium- and long-term economic planning, and policy frameworks being developed by Iranian policy groups, including a critical analysis of the Iran Prosperity Project.

The discussion will be introduced by Claudia Yaghoobi, Director of the UNC Center for Middle East and Islamic Studies and moderated by Mehdi Shadmehr, professor in the UNC Department of Public Policy. Panelists include: Mohammad Jahan-Parvar, a DC-based economist, Mehdi Beyhaghi, a DC-based economist, Borghan Narajabad, a DC-based economist, and Soroush Ghazi Kalahroudi of the University of Alabama.

title: Weekday Carillon Recital
description:

The recital begins with an opening excerpt of music, then a bell tolls five times to mark the hour, and then the carillonneur plays a selection of hymns and other songs appropriate for the day.

Listen to the recital anywhere outside on West Campus or online via the livestream on the Duke Chapel Facebook page: https://www.facebook.com/DukeChapel.

title: [Deep Canvass Institute] Skills Session 3: Identifying & Processing Underlying Conflict
description:
How to join:
You will be asked to complete a brief Zoom registration at the time you log-in for the event.

In conversations, we spend a lot of time on the surface, arguing about facts and opinions we’ve heard online , but never really understand the underlying emotional conflicts that drive our beliefs and the beliefs of others. Join us to learn more about identifying core underlying conflicts, and how to approach processing those conflicts by focusing on the things we’ve actually experienced rather than what we’ve heard on the news.

*This session will be offered twice - please choose the date/time that works best for you.*

title: Roots and Routes
description:
Join the Adopted Students Union for Roots and Routes, a March 9 event that will be all about reflection and reconnection. We encourage participants to fully embrace where they came from and to create a visual or mood board that offers a glimpse into who they are and what represents their “lost culture.” The goal is to foster a judgment-free space where everyone feels comfortable expressing themselves and reconnecting with parts of their identity. Please create a physical or digital visual/mood board before our March 9 event. During the event, you can share your board. If you'd just like to visit and connect with others without creating a mood board, you can do so as well.

Free snacks will be provided. With St. Patrick’s Day right around the corner, you’ll also get to build your own “luck bag" and decorate it with markers and other craft supplies.

title: Solving Sustainability Live Podcast: Future of Protein
location: Partners I – NC State University, Raleigh Founded (Suite 1650)
description:
Solving Sustainability is a live interdisciplinary podcast that seeks to raise awareness about macro-level sustainability challenges and innovations while fostering meaningful discussion between students, researchers, entrepreneurs and policymakers.

The third episode of the podcast, "Future Sources of Protein," will delve into the scientific innovations, environmental trade-offs and economic considerations shaping alternative protein systems. The discussion will focus on plant-based foods, precision fermentation and cultivated meat and how they will shape the future of sustainable food production. The guests on the podcast will be:

Rohan Shirwaiker, co-director of the Bezos CenterRody Hawkins, CEO of Improved NatureAjinkya Arun Atkare, co-president of the Alt Protein Project at NC State

description:

Bring games, friends, and a welcoming spirit. We're excited to have you with us!

title: Ackland Film Forum: "Boyhood" (Richard Linklater, 2014)
location: The Lumina Theater, 620 Market Street, Chapel Hill, 27516, United States
description:
Filmed over twelve years with the same cast members who conspicuously age from segment to segment, Boyhood offers one of cinema’s most literal and radical explorations of coming of age. Following Mason (Ellar Coltrane) from early childhood through his first days of college, the film refuses traditional narrative climaxes in favor of accumulation: small moments, shifting family dynamics, and incremental changes in perception as time itself figures as the “main character.” Linklater’s method allows us to experience maturation as something lived, uneven, and quietly profound.
The film will be introduced by Rick Warner, Bowman and Gordon Gray Distinguished Term Professor and Director of Film Studies.

This film will be screened at 7:30 p.m. at The Lumina Theater (620 Market St, Chapel Hill, NC 27516).
Tickets are free for all audience members, and registration is required via The Lumina’s webpage. Please click here to reserve your free ticket. UNC-Chapel Hill students can receive CLE credit for attending by scanning the QR code at the event.

About the Series:
Join the Ackland Film Forum this spring for Growing Up, Looking Back, a series that accompanies the Ackland exhibition Bill Bamberger: Boys Will Be Men. This semester’s series highlights the filmgoing experience by using three local movie theaters as its venues.
This film series is dedicated to the memory of Allison Portnow Lathrop, former Head of Public Programs at the Ackland Art Museum. For more than a decade, Allison was an indispensable champion of film culture on campus and in Chapel Hill, most notably through her visionary stewardship of the Ackland Film Forum. She fostered a spirit of generous collaboration among faculty, students, and artists. Her belief in the power of cinema lives on in the conversations and shared experiences she helped create.
The Ackland Film Forum is co-organized by the Ackland Art Museum and UNC Film Studies, housed in the UNC-Chapel Hill Department of English and Comparative Literature.

title: Landon Conrath: The North American Tour 2026
location: Motorco, 723 Rigsbee, Durham, NC, 27701, United States
description:
with Abby Holiday!
In a nutshell, Landon Conrath’s success story is: “It worked, so we tried it again.” A Minneapolis-based, music-making force, Conrath has gotten far with this mantra over the past four years. Since handing off the drumsticks in local rock band Harbor and Home to take center stage as a solo artist, Conrath has written and released 40-plus songs.
In 2024, the prolific artist put out the album Employee of the Year and Songs of a Past Life EP. His adeptly produced, melodically catchy songs accompany lyrics that hit home with the day-to-day experience of living in your 20s, especially regarding friendship, love, and heartbreak.
Today, Conrath has amassed tens of millions of Spotify streams, and toured the U.S. and Europe. At the start of 2025, he kicked off a 7th Street Entry residency, which continues on Jan. 9 and 16. Conrath spoke in detail with The Current about how “just a dude from Lino Lakes” became a local hit-making machine, including his origin story, songwriting process, collaborators, inspirations, and more.

location: I-147 Overpass @ Anderson Street, 121 Anderson St, Durham, NC, 27705

We are there to demonstrate in defense of our democracy and the rights of all in this country. We are for raising the minimum wage, housing for all, healthcare for all, protecting the earth, and protecting our workers. Come any time during this time. We have a mighty crew who comes each week. title: 2026 UNC Cleantech Summit
description:
The 12th annual UNC Cleantech Summit will take place at the UNC Friday Conference Center on March 9 – 10, 2026. The two-day event will bring together professionals in business, government, and academia from across the Southeast and around the world for discussion, workshops, mentoring, and networking to foster leadership and growth in the Southeast’s cleantech economy. The Summit convenes more than 1,000 attendees and is the largest event of its kind by a university in the United States. Co-hosted by the UNC Institue for the Environment and the Ackerman Center for Excellence in Sustainability at the Kenan-Flagler Business School.

Register here: https://web.cvent.com/event/dc0e55ed-323b-4ce9-bb78-8699d3e904f3/summary?RefId=UNC%20Website

title: Duke Ortho Departmental Research Day
description:
We are delighted to invite you to the 2026 Duke Orthopaedics Departmental Research Day & Symposium: Aligning Science and Society. This annual event showcases the wide range, depth, and impact of research within the Duke Orthopaedics Department-from basic science to clinical advances and community-engaged projects.

This year's program has been expanded to highlight the full scope of scholarly activities in our department. We look forward to your participation in celebrating the work of our investigators, trainees, and staff as they advance orthopaedic science and enhance musculoskeletal health.

The event includes:
- Short Talks from undergraduate, professional, and graduate students; clinical and research fellows; APPs; clinic staff; administrators; and faculty-all illustrating the diversity of inquiry in orthopaedics.
- Resident Presentations featuring outstanding research led by residents.
- Poster Sessions displaying innovative work across basic, translational, clinical, and population health research related to musculoskeletal care.

Join us for a day of discovery, collaboration, and celebration as we showcase the incredible breadth of research across our department.

title: Duke Pediatrics Grand Rounds – "Immune Dysregulation"
description:
Title of Presentation: "Immune Dysregulation"

The Duke Department of Pediatrics holds its weekly Grand Rounds lecture on Tuesday mornings from 8 to 9 a.m. | Lectures are delivered to an in-person audience in Conference Room DHN 2002 as well as virtually via Zoom. Physicians who attend Grand Rounds are eligible to receive CME credit.

title: Hematology Case Conference: “The Joys of Gemcitabine”
description:
This weekly conference is held each Tuesday from 8-9 a.m. and includes case-based presentations on a wide variety of topics in hematology.
In general, discussion will cover inpatient and outpatient problems, with an emphasis on including primary radiographic data, clinical laboratory results and pathology specimens for review. Several recent articles relevant to the discussion will be presented with each case, with an emphasis on recent research (clinical, translational and basic).

title: Social Media Made Simple: Tools, Timing, and Templates
description: Social media doesn't have to feel like a second full-time job. Join us for Social Media Made Simple, led by the always dynamic Kim Kaid of Kaid Marketing. Kim brings the perfect mix of strategy, creativity, and real-world practicality. (and our attendees consistently rave about her sessions) In this interactive workshop, you'll learn: Which platforms actually make sense for your business (hint: not all of them) How to create eye-catching graphics using simple, customizable templates What to post, when to post, and how to stay consistent without burning out Kim will break it all down in a way that's current, clear, and immediatey actionable. You'll leave with practical tools, renewed confidence, and a realistic plan that makes social media work for your business... not the other way around. If you've ever thought "I know I should be doing more online, but I don't know where to start," this class is for you.

title: CaSA Conversations: Campus Nature and Health
description:
Please join NC State's Climate and Sustainability Academy (CaSA) for the second event in our three-part CaSA Conversations series. Join us for coffee, breakfast and networking from 8:45 to 9 a.m., followed by a panel discussion on campus nature and health.

Dawn Rodriguez-Ward, an academic program manager at the Climate and Sustainability Academy and an Environmental First Year Program instructor, will moderate the panel discussion. The panelists are:

Megan Lupek, an associate teaching professor and the co-director of the Environmental Sciences ProgramTommy Holden, teaching professor and coordinator of the Outdoor Leadership minorDr. Courtney Hinton, campus medical directorThis event is free to attend, but registration is required.

title: Harriet Jacobs in the Attic
description:
Join us for an immersive audiovisual experience as we illuminate Harriet Jacobs, a fugitive North Carolina enslaved person who hid in a tiny attic garret for seven years. While hiding she planned her escape, fled to the North, and eventually became a key figure in the abolitionist movement.

In celebration of Black History Month, we invite you to explore a to-scale recreation of Harriet’s attic space and listen to readings from her book, Incidents in the Life of a Slave Girl, which was published in 1861.

title: Immunology Seminar Series - Elizabeth Jones, PhD: "Disability and Inclusive Excellence: "Definitions, context, and best practices"
description:
Elizabeth Jones, PhD

Director, Education and Outreach

Duke Office for Institutional Equity

"Disability and Inclusive Excellence: Definitions, context, and best practices"

This interactive session will discuss how disability shows up in the workplace and how we can ensure that everyone in our community is supported in their work. Participants will be invited to explore disability as a form of human difference that touches all of our lives. We will discuss disability history, physical disability, neurodiversity, increasing access, and more. Participants will engage activities to deepen their knowledge about disability, and their capacity to cultivate an ability inclusive workforce.

Host: Saskia Hemmers, PhD

title: Lake Raleigh Woods Hike
description:
Please join us on a monthly basis for a walk through the Lake Raleigh Woods, one of NC State's hidden gems and Hallowed Spaces. The hike is about 1.5 miles. Below are a few things to remember for the hike:

Make sure to park in the northwest corner of the Park Alumni Center parking lot a little before 9 a.m. for a brief safety conversation. Parking is free for everyone. Wear closed-toed shoes and bring a water bottle.Check the weather forecast and wear weather-appropriate clothing on the hike.Restrooms are available in the Alumni Center. There are no facilities on the trail.The hike will last approximately 90 minutes but could be a little shorter depending on the group's pace. Space is limited for each hike. Be sure to register.

title: The Road Through “Don’t Ask, Don’t Tell”
description:
The Road Through Don’t Ask, Don’t Tell by photographer, oral historian, and U.S. Marine Corps veteran Theresa Scott. This deeply personal documentary exhibition explores the lived experiences of LGBTQIA+ veterans who served under restrictive military policies.
Through intimate portrait photography paired with first-person oral histories, Scott brings forward stories long silenced by institutional policy and cultural pressure. Drawing on her own service, she offers viewers an unflinching look at the realities faced by service members who were expected to serve with honor while hiding essential parts of who they were.
Exhibition Dates: February 1 – April 26, 2026
Artist Talk & Reception: March 26, 5:30–7:00 p.m.

title: Election Polling: Design, Analysis, and Communication
description: As the 2026 election cycle approaches, polls will once again dominate headlines and shape public discussion. This half-day course provides a practical foundation in the design, analysis, and communication of election polls. Topics include questionnaire design, the advantages and limitations of different survey modes, weighting techniques to improve representativeness, strategies for likely voter screens, and approaches to assess, interpret, and communicate poll results accurately. Registration and fee (refundable for students) are required for Zoom link access.

location: North Carolina Museum of Art, 2110 Blue Ridge Rd, Raleigh, 27607, United States
description:
Join us as we seek comfort in nature and connection with others as we walk the paths at the Museum Park at the North Carolina Museum of Art.
Note: The Park paths are paved with slight inclines. Please leave pets at home. You are welcome to attend some or all of the walking sessions.
Registration required. Call 919-719-7199.

title: Legends & Landmarks: Paintings and Drawings by James C. Horton
description: In commemoration of America's 250th, the Bellamy Mansion Museum is hosting an art exhibition by local artist James C. Horton, which includes over 40 original paintings and drawings that highlight historic scenes and places from around the region and country that help tell America's story. Visitors can view the exhibit free of charge any time the museum is open (M-Sun. 10 am-4 pm). Please stop by the Carriage ​House Visitor Center to gain admission. Art is available for purchase and an Artist's Reception will be held on February 27th (6-8pm). Visit bellamymansion.org/exhibits for more information or email info@bellamymansion.org if you have questions.

title: Mobile Health Assessment
description: Health Assessment is a free, confidential health screening provided through Duke benefits. It includes total cholesterol, blood pressure, blood glucose, BMI, waist circumference height, weight and body mass index measurements. Additionally, there is a goal setting and referral session with a nurse, dietitian, or fitness specialist. Many participants find the coaching session helps them get started making lifestyle changes and connects them to LIVE FOR LIFE and community resources to support them. Health Assessments are available to all active Duke employees.

title: Women at the Edge of War: Carolina Piedmont's Heroines of the American Revolution
description:
KMHM is excited to present Women at the Edge of War: Carolina Piedmont's Heroines of the American Revolution. This exhibit highlights the daily courage and civic work of women in the Carolinas, whose efforts were essential to Revolutionary resistance and survival on the southern frontier.

The exhibit will run from Feb. 17, 2026 to May 30, 2026. The museum is open Tues-Sat, 10-4. Admission is free. Check out kingsmountainmuseum.org or call 704-739-1019 for more information.

location: Prairie Ridge, 1671 Gold Star Drive, Raleigh, NC, 27607, United States
description:
Join us as we explore art outdoors, mixing natural materials with traditional art disciplines, testing new nature-based art techniques, and using inspiration from nature to create artistic masterpieces.
Each day will feature a different outdoor art activity – today’s activity is creating nature wands! Join us as we use natural materials to make a wand to take along on your magical outdoor explorations!
This drop-in program is designed for kids ages 7 and under and occurs outdoors from 10:30am – 11:30am at the picnic tables below the Nature PlaySpace. All children must be accompanied by an adult. Come dressed for the weather and prepared to get messy! Art Outdoors is a free program, with a suggested donation of $5/child. Make your donation here. For more information, contact prairieridge@naturalsciences.org.

title: Voltage Control's FREE Facilitation Lab RDU
location: BaySix: Space for Good, 414 Dupont Cir, Raleigh, 27603
description:
Facilitation isn’t mastered by reading about it — it’s built through practice. Facilitation Lab RDU is an interactive session designed to help you sharpen your skills in real time, alongside others who care about meaningful collaboration.
This lab centers on the Practice Playground format, where participants actively engage in facilitating, role-playing, observing, and offering feedback. Instead of discussing theory, you’ll work directly with real challenges in a supportive and structured environment.
Bring something you’re currently navigating — a workshop you’re refining, a team that feels stuck, or a moment you want to handle differently next time. Through guided exercises and peer input, you’ll experiment with approaches, gain new perspectives, and build confidence in your facilitation choices.
Join a community of practitioners committed to creating stronger conversations, better collaboration, and more impactful group experiences.

title: Lunch and Learn: The Path to Becoming AI-First: An Operator’s Playbook
description:
All Things AI (Live)
What happens when a recruiting operator — not a software engineer — decides to rebuild his company around AI?

In this live lunch & learn session, James Horne, Director of Delivery at Axiom Path, shares how his team built 25+ AI automations and digital employees that now operate alongside human recruiters — screening candidates, managing workflows, and turning 21,000+ historical recruiting notes into a searchable knowledge system.

James will demonstrate a live voice AI built to talk about...itself and it's role in Axiom Path and their AI Transformation.

This session explores what it really means to become AI-first: starting with process, increasing leverage, and learning how to manage digital employees alongside human employees.

This is not a coding talk. It’s an operator’s blueprint.

title: Meditation + Yoga Flow with Recess Yoga
location: Recess Yoga Durham, 345 W. Main st, 2nd floor, Durham, NC, Durham, 27701
description:
If you’ve been running on auto-pilot lately, this class is your pause. Join Diamond (she/her) for a 45-minute midday retreat of mind-body connection, gentle yogic movement, and a practice of meditation. This practice is located at Recess Yoga Durham. Please note the studio is accessible by stairs on the second floor.
Connect with Diamond on Instagram: https://www.instagram.com/carolinad1m3/
Over the past decade, Diamond (she/her) has explored several paths of yoga, leading to her completion of a 200-hour in-person Hatha/Raja Yoga & Meditation teacher training in Durham, NC in 2024. Her training follows the lineages of Sri Dharma Mittra and Thich Nhat Hanh, with a focus on mindfulness, meditation, and inclusivity.
Her teaching style is gentle and empowering, encouraging students to listen to their bodies, honor their needs, and explore the practice at their own pace. She believes yoga is a powerful tool for managing everyday stress and deepening self-awareness, both on and off the mat. Off the mat, Diamond works in hospitality as a restaurant manager in downtown Durham and enjoys exploring local food, nature, and live music. Whether you’re brand new or returning to the mat, Diamond invites you to join her in a practice rooted in presence, curiosity, and self-discovery.

title: Free Yoga
location: Partners I – NC State University, Suite 2580
description: Take a break from your day and enjoy a free yoga class on Centennial Campus. Classes are held every weekday, except when the university is closed, from 12:15 to 1:05 p.m. The classes are open to everyone, and registration is not required. Please bring your own mat.

title: State of AI : Context Engineering with Microsoft and Anthropic
description:
Modern Web (Triangle)
Context is becoming a defining layer of modern AI systems and is shaping how models reason, respond, and integrate into real products. This event explores how context engineering is influencing the current state of AI and the way teams are thinking about building more capable and reliable systems.

[Join us for a conversation](https://luma.com/79w48jgi) with experts from Microsoft and Anthropic as we examine emerging approaches to structuring context, lessons from real-world AI development, and the considerations guiding what comes next.

Featuring insights from
[Harald Kirschner](https://www.linkedin.com/in/digitarald/), Principal Product Manager for GitHub Copilot and VS Code at Microsoft
[Ado Kukic](https://www.linkedin.com/in/adocomplete/), Community Manager at Anthropic
[Brandon Mathis](https://www.linkedin.com/in/mathisbrandon/), Engineering Lead, This Dot Labs
[Tracy Lee](https://www.linkedin.com/in/tracyslee/), CEO, This Dot Labs

[If you are interested in attending, please RSVP using this link!](https://luma.com/iho7wvqp)

title: Creating Your Own Digital Exhibit
description:
Want to tell a story using images, videos, documents, or other media from your research project? You can create and host a digital exhibit fore free using CollectionBuilder! This workshop will cover how to set up your own digital exhibit using CollectionBuilder and Google Sheets. Attendees will learn how to add images, PDFs, videos, and more to their website. CollectionBuilder makes it easy to set up your own digital exhibit with minimal technical expertise.

This workshop is offered by the University Libraries and will be led by librarian Rolando Rodriguez and CALA Shannon Henry.

This workshop is being offered remotely via Zoom only. Registrants will receive a Zoom link; those on the waitlist will not receive a Zoom link unless they are bumped up to a registered spot. After the workshop, registrants and those on the waitlist will receive a link to the workshop recording. You can register on the University Libraries calendar: https://calendar.lib.unc.edu/event/16023378

Guided tours of Fort Macon are available daily at 1:00 p.m. during the Park's slower season between mid Oct. and Mid April.

The tours will last about 45 minutes.

Meet at the fort entrance. Pets are not allowed inside the fort.

Photo by C. Peek: The fort at Fort Macon State Park

title: River Cane Restoration (Raleigh)
description:
Join NCWF South Wake Conservationists Chapter and Raleigh Parks for a river cane restoration workday! River cane, a native bamboo, is a woody grass native to floodplains of the southeastern US. River cane is an ecologically and culturally important plant that helps improve water quality and prevent erosion by stabilizing the banks of waterways. This plant is also used for basket making and other tribal arts. Unfortunately, river cane now occupies only 2% of its former extent in the US! Join us as we protect river cane by removing invasive plants from around the canebreaks at Walnut Creek Wetland Center.
REGISTER HERE

Drop in and reframe your day with calm and clarity.

title: Right to Be's Stand Up Against Street Harassment
description:
REGISTER HERE: https://righttobe.org/events/stand-up-against-street-harassment-44/

How can we take action if we don’t know what to do? Right To Be is offering this free, one-hour, interactive training to train people on how to safely intervene when you witness street harassment — or experience it yourself. During this training you’ll learn a clear, adaptable, and expert-approved set of tools that have been proven to reduce the prevalence of street harassment: Right To Be’s 5Ds of bystander intervention.
We’ll start by deepening our understanding of street harassment and its impact. Then, we’ll talk through five strategies for intervention: distract, delegate, document, delay, and direct; and how to prioritize your own safety while intervening. We’ll also talk about what to do if you’re worried the violence will turn on you and how to avoid escalating the situation. Then, we’ll talk about what to do if you experience harassment and give you tools to safely get your power back.
We’ll have time at the end for practice. Exit polls show 98.8% of attendees leave the training feeling like there is at least one thing they can do the next time they witness harassment.

title: Webinar: From Uprising to Airstrikes: What Do We Miss About Iran?
description:
This panel brings together scholars of Iranian Studies to examine the unfolding crisis in Iran, from the nationwide protests and violent state crackdown in January 2026 to the escalating regional war that followed. The discussion asks what is often overlooked when Iran is framed primarily through the lens of geopolitics or military confrontation. Panelists will explore how domestic unrest, political repression, economic pressures, sanctions, and regional rivalries intersect in shaping Iran’s current trajectory. By placing recent events in historical and political context, the panel highlights the perspectives of Iranian society and considers what these developments may mean for Iran’s future and the broader Middle East.

The discussion will be moderated by Claudia Yaghoobi, Director of the UNC Center for Middle East and Islamic Studies. Panelists include: Janet Afary, Distinguished Professor of History at the University of California, Santa Barbara; Mina Khanlarzadeh, Visiting Faculty and Historian at Deep Springs College; Afshin Matin-Asgari, Professor of History at California State University, Los Angeles; and Arash Davari, Assistant Professor of Political Science at the University of Minnesot.

Register at: https://go.unc.edu/cmeis3

title: Weekday Carillon Recital
description:

title: Durham Social Hack Night
description:
Triangle Developers
Hey tech folks, come out and hang at Hi-Wire starting at 6pm! Hack Night is meant to be a social evening for folks in tech. It's less about networking and more about community.

**Look for the Hack Night tabletop sign** and nerds wearing name tags to find us. If the weather is nice, we'll probably be outside.

Help folks feel welcome by using the **"Pac Man Rule"** \- when standing with a group of people \(usually in a circle\)\, leave some space open so that another person can easily join\.

See our [Code of Conduct](https://github.com/TriangleDevsSlack/policies/blob/master/code-of-conduct.md). To summarize: be nice or leave. No tolerance for harassment or oppression. In addition, since alcohol is present at these events, please be mindful of your consumption and respect other people's choices on whether or not to consume.

**FAQs**
*Do folks hack at Hack Night? Should I bring my computer?*
This is more of a night for hackers than an actual hack night. You're welcome to bring a laptop, but most people will only be socializing.

*What are the beverage options? Are there any non-alcoholic drinks?*
Hi-Wire Brewing offers many types of beers including a few non-alcoholic options plus wine and some liquor. They usually have a happy hour special on the first few beers on the menu. In the same complex are Cugino Forno which has additional drink options, and Two Roosters which has excellent ice cream.

*Are there any drink specials?*
If Hack Night is on a Tuesday, Hi-Wire usually has $5 pints for their flagship beers. Look for the list at the counter.

*Do people eat snacks/dinner during the meetup?*
Sometimes folks will order a pizza or salad from Cugino Forno. I sometimes grab an ice cream from Two Roosters across the way. Most people eat before or after but it's no big deal - whatever you prefer and are most comfortable with.

*How can I connect with the developer community?*
Go to [triangledevelopers.com](https://triangledevelopers.com/) to find the Discord and Slack info.

title: *IN-PERSON* SF/F Group
description:
Durham Writers Group
We will be at the People's Solidarity Hub at 1809 Chapel Hill Rd, Durham. This venue does not serve food or drink, but feel free to bring your own. There is a kitchenette for our use, but be sure to clean up afterwards. The space might be a little cozy; please consider masking or staying home if you have a respiratory ailment.

This sub-group focuses on Speculative Fiction, including Science Fiction, Fantasy and Horror. If you have anything that falls under that category — be it short story, poem, novel-in-progress or screenplay — then come! We'd love to hear from you.

Our format allows each reader 15 minutes to present their work aloud, followed by approximately 20 minutes of feedback from the other attendees. To ensure fairness, each attendee is limited to 1 minute of Initial Feedback time. During that period, the author remains silent. Next, we have a 5 minute Free-For-All discussion about the work -- then the author may speak. (We usually get through three readers.)

Though not required, in order to receive more in-depth feed-back, readers may post their work in advance on our Google Group:
https://groups.google.com/d/forum/durham-sff-writers-group

We ask that participants keep their comments constructive and respectful, focusing on what works or doesn't work in the piece.

We also ask that readers provide a warning, both on the Google Group and during the meeting, if their story features content that some readers might find upsetting. This includes descriptions of violence or sexual content.

Readers are:
• Sagar
• Zack
• Sigma

Backup Readers are:
• Crystina
• Ruth
• Allegra

Deep Backup:
• Milbre

***

***We meet every Tuesday at the same time, but alternate between In-Person*** ***and on Zoom. Members may attend both formats, if desired, but readers will be listed separately under the In-Person or Zoom weeks.***

title: after work bike ride on the ATT
description:
Triangle Hiking and Outdoors Group
I know that it’s hard to imagine, given the weather we’ve had from. Mid January to almost mid February, but it’s time to prepare for the 2026 season of the Tuesday after work bike rides. This seasonal, weekly event has been going on since 2011! It will begin with just over 1 hour of riding, and as the days get longer we’ll increase the ride time and distance tracking sunset.
We'll be starting near Southpoint and final starting point will be sent out Monday evening. Make sure that your bike is in good working order - tires hold air and are up to pressure, brakes and shifters work and your chain has been oiled. We'll be riding just about 1 hours and about 12 miles. The ride is fairly flat and shaded and our pace will be moderate (about 13 mph average). I recommend a hard tail or hybrid bike, but if you really want to bring a road bike it's ok, though you'll likely be a bit bored. Unicycles....? I'll be eating after the ride at a restaurant. You're welcome to join me if you like. Helmets are required and by signing up you agree to THOG liability waiver statement. Please Note!! Any cancellation less than 30 hours before the beginning of the event will be considered a "no show"

description:

Beer and Banjos is a free, family-friendly evening of roots music every Tuesday at Raleigh’s Bowstring Pizza and Brewyard. Hosted by the mighty local musicians known collectively as the Beer and Banjos Allstars, these weekly concerts feature guest musicians and take place on Bowstring’s spacious patio when the weather allows, or inside by the bar when rain, heat, or cold threaten the vibe.
Tips for the musicians are gratefully accepted (look for a helper walking around with a bucket for cash and QR codes for online tips), and don’t forget to check out the merch table. Bowstring serves tasty pizza and other food as well as cold beer and other beverages.
Learn more about Beer and Banjos events statewide here.

title: Pony Ride with Ponysaurus Brewing Co.
location: Ponysaurus Brewing Co., 219 Hood Street, Durham, 27701
description:
The Pony Ride is a social, leisure bike ride that begins at 6:30pm at The Bull in CCB Plaza in Downtown Durham (201 Corcoran St, Durham, NC 27701). Gather at 6:30pm at the Bull in downtown Durham. Ride should be rolling out by 7pm and will end at Ponysaurus Brewing Co. Route will take about 1 hour. Ride distance: 5-8 miles (leisurely pace).
Enter for a chance to win beer prizes – get your free raffle ticket at the Bull before the ride and be present at Ponysaurus at the end of the ride to win. Limit one ticket per participant.
The goal of The Pony Ride is to raise awareness of cycling and cyclists on the road in Durham, foster the cycling community and provide a fun easygoing social environment for people to come together, ride bikes and enjoy great beers, good food, and Downtown Durham. Celebrate the awesome city we live in, the great people that live here, and the joy we all get from getting outside and riding bikes! This is a social ride, and riders of all levels are welcome!

title: Public School Strong Organizing Meeting
description:
Link to join: https://www.mobilize.us/join/46979984/1:8a82c476b1f77694f639474ce602665b2e94401cf3d9fcadb88b6bd1bb557fc1/

How to join:
Hi there,

Thanks for signing up for our Public School Strong Orientation & Organizing Meeting!

Be sure to register on zoom in advance for this meeting:
https://raceforward-org.zoom.us/meeting/register/tZIvcuiurz4pE9BGsfJi-mBBX_lhl2bPG7Ti

After registering, you will receive a confirmation email containing information about joining the meeting.

**We are thrilled to welcome David Backer, author of As Public As Possible: Radical Finance for America’s Public Schools, as our guest speaker at January’s meeting.**

Join us for the next Public Schools Strong organizing meeting on Thursday, January 22nd, where parents, family members, educators, and supporters from across the nation gather each month to gain practical tools to promote, protect, and strengthen our public schools.

*If you have already attended a Public School Strong Orientation, you'll join a breakout room with current members for connection, reflection and training. If you're new, we've got you covered too!*

**Do you want honest, equitable and fully-funded public schools?** Acting locally is key.

### Join public school families, educators and supporters from across the country to learn how to:
1. Form a Public School Strong team in your school district
2. Engage your community to get school boards and district leaders to speak up for equitable and fully funded public schools
3. Connect your local advocacy to others for state and national impact

For more info, please visit publicschoolstrong.org

### Current Public School Strong Members

There is strength in staying connected. After completing Public School Strong (PSS) Orientation, this is your consistent space to reconnect with your peers across the country to:

1. Access helpful tools and practice relevant skills,
2. Connect the dots between what is happening locally and nationally,
3. Learn from mistakes and progress made along the way

Bring a friend!

**Self Directed Orientation Now Available!**

If these live training times don't work for you, you can complete your orientation at your own pace:

1) Create Account in NEA Portal (open to non-NEA members too):

neapartnera.learnupon.com/users/sign_up

2) Select the PSS orientation by clicking on this link:

bit.ly/SelfDirectedPSS

How to prepare:
### Hi there! Thanks for signing up for a 'Public School Strong Organizing Meeting!' (for new folks we'll also have a new member orientation)

This event is virtual, will provide updates on the federal attacks on our public schools and the immigrant community, actions you can take right now and the tools to support you in doing son.

For new folks, we'll provide an introduction to our Public School Strong campaign. Check out our organizing platform using this link to find or create a local team:

- publicschoolstrong.org

More on this powerful tool at your Orientation...

https://www.mobilize.us/raceforward/event/596321/

title: Wake Audubon Meeting: Wake Audubon Meeting:  The Purple Martin Story: Their Journey, Their Future, and Our Role
location: Nature Research Center, 121 W Jones St, Raleigh, NC 27603, USA
description:
Wake Audubon Monthly Meeting: The Purple Martin Story: Their Journey, Their Future, and Our Role
(Meeting is usually In Person and Zoom, but check back for updates!)
Time: Second Tuesday of the month, 7:30 - 9:00 pm
Nature Research Center, 121 W. Jones St, Raleigh, NC 27601
Topic: This presentation will explore the biology and history of the Purple Martin, the conservation challenges impacting the species, effective techniques for colony attraction and management, and opportunities for community involvement in local Purple Martin projects.
Speaker: Certified Environmental Educator Courtney Rousseau is the President and co-founder of the North Carolina Purple Martin Society, which was established in 2010 and incorporated as a nonprofit in 2021. Since 2008, she has worked with multiple martin colony sites, helped to coordinate annual banding events in Raleigh, and mentored new martin landlords, while collaborating with birding and wildlife groups across the state. Her conservation and educational work has been recognized with awards from the Wake Audubon Society and South Wake Conservationists.
https://www.ncpurplemartin.org/

Place: In-person + Virtual via Zoom
Location: NC Museum of Natural Sciences: Nature Research Center, 121 W Jones St, Raleigh, NC - 4th floor meeting room
Location: In-person + Zoom (Note: The two speakers will be on Zoom )
Place: In-person + Virtual - use https://www.zoom.us/join , choose "Join meeting" (no login needed)
Meeting code: 831 3786 5298
Password: 4Birds
Park (for free after 6:30 pm) and walk to the main museum entrance on Jones Street, just to the right of the giant globe. Since the NC Museum of Natural Sciences is usually closed at night, someone will be at the door ONLY from 7 to 7:45 to let you in, so please be on time. Take the elevator to the fourth floor meeting room. The east-facing entrance of the "old wing" of the museum will not be used. If you feel unwell, please stay home and enjoy our presentation over Zoom
We hope you will help us by joining Wake Audubon and National Audubon!
visit wakeaudubon.org

title: NHBA Bird Outing
description:
Contact David Anderson at d47anders@gmail.com with questions. The location will be Brumley North: 3620 Old State Hwy 10, Chapel Hill, NC 27514.

This outing is sponsored by the New Hope Bird Alliance (formerly New Hope Audubon).

Outings usually last 2-3 hours. No reservations or sign-up needed. If there is a large turnout for any particular outing, we may split into two groups. We do recommend carpooling for families or friend pods; while we love pets, there is a ‘no-pet’ policy for bird outings. New birders and birders of all skill levels are welcome.

Please wear long pants, socks, and sturdy shoes (ticks and chiggers may be present). Sun and bug protection are also recommended. Bring snacks and water if you like. Heavy rain will result in cancellation.

title: Exploring Coastal Habitats on the Rachel Carson Reserve
location: North Carolina Maritime Museum in Beaufort
description:
Join a member of our education team to discover the various plants and animals of the salt marsh and tidal flats at the Rachel Carson Reserve. The guided hike will take you through the different habitats and the sandy, muddy, and wet terrain found on Town Marsh and Bird Shoal. The program is recommended for ages 12 and up. All participants under 18 must be accompanied by an adult.

Field programs are designed to give participants hands-on experiences with North Carolina’s environment, history, and culture. Please note that these programs take place primarily outside. Please review the age and experience requirements before registering. Pre-registration is required by noon the day prior to the program. For more information, call 252-504-7758.

title: Harriet Jacobs in the Attic
description:
Join us for an immersive audiovisual experience as we illuminate Harriet Jacobs, a fugitive North Carolina enslaved person who hid in a tiny attic garret for seven years. While hiding she planned her escape, fled to the North, and eventually became a key figure in the abolitionist movement.

In celebration of Black History Month, we invite you to explore a to-scale recreation of Harriet’s attic space and listen to readings from her book, Incidents in the Life of a Slave Girl, which was published in 1861.

title: Lifelong Hike: Raven Rock
description:
Raven Rock State Park is a great spot to enjoy a piedmont hardwood forest and view the Cape Fear River from Raven Rock, a 150-foot-high geologic feature for which the park was named. The park has a diverse topography, including high bluffs and low flood plains along the Cape Fear River. We’ll hike the 3-mile Raven Rock Loop Trail keeping an eye out for spring wildflowers along the way.
Please note: You will be required to enter the following information for each registrant before you can complete your registration: emergency contact, physician contact and health information (allergies, conditions, medications relevant to this field experience). Take a moment to gather this information before beginning your registration.
REGISTER FOR THIS TRIP
If the trip is full, please email Missy Chernick to be added to the waitlist.
Registration deadline: Monday, March 9 at 9:00 AM
For additional information about this trip and other Lifelong Hikes & Adventures, contact Jerry Reynolds at 919.707.9884.

title: The Road Through “Don’t Ask, Don’t Tell”
description:
The Road Through Don’t Ask, Don’t Tell by photographer, oral historian, and U.S. Marine Corps veteran Theresa Scott. This deeply personal documentary exhibition explores the lived experiences of LGBTQIA+ veterans who served under restrictive military policies.
Through intimate portrait photography paired with first-person oral histories, Scott brings forward stories long silenced by institutional policy and cultural pressure. Drawing on her own service, she offers viewers an unflinching look at the realities faced by service members who were expected to serve with honor while hiding essential parts of who they were.
Exhibition Dates: February 1 – April 26, 2026
Artist Talk & Reception: March 26, 5:30–7:00 p.m.

title: Culture and Communication I: Perception, understanding, and connection
description:
This session will cover general information about the ways in which our culture(s) inform our understanding of ourselves and each other, as well as how to improve our capacity and skills for engaging across difference. This interactive session includes opportunities for individual exploration and relational skill building. Participants will be invited to think about how each of us forms our understandings, and how we can understand how others do as well. With increased understanding, participants will be invited to practice communication strategies to be thoughtful and effective in their interactions and relationships in the workplace. Participants should be prepared to engage in discussion in this session.

Facilitated by: Elizabeth Jones, Ph.D., Director for Education and Outreach

Wednesday, March 11, 2026 | 9:30 AM - 11:30 AM | REGISTER HERE

Registrants will receive additional information about the location, including parking, prior to the session. Please contact oie-education@duke.edu if you have any questions or concerns.

title: Watauga Livestake Giveaway (Boone)
description:
Live stakes are cuttings from dormant trees that can grow into new plants along river or stream banks for riparian buffer restoration. In the winter, trees go into dormancy, a natural process where they conserve energy to survive the cold. This is the perfect time for planting live stakes, as the cuttings can focus on growing strong roots before the spring season arrives.
The live staking season typically runs from November to March, where you can plant hardy hardwood stakes that will establish their roots first. These roots stay steady, quietly working underground, and when spring arrives, the stakes burst into life, growing their branches and leaves.
We will be offering bundles of 50 stakes with varieties ranging from Silky Dogwood, Silky Willow, Ninebark, and Elderberry. Instructions on planting will be provided. Live stakes and educational packets are free, but to ensure we have enough live stakes sign up is required.
These live stake giveaways are a partnership between North Carolina Wildlife Federation High Country Wild Chapter, New River Conservancy, Blue Ridge RC&D, and Partners for Fish and Wildlife with support from local Soil and Water Conservation District, Cooperative Extension offices and National Wildlife Federation’s Trees for Wildlife Program

LEARN MORE

description:

This training will discuss the importance of not being completely sedentary all day, and review practical, easy ways to take care of your body while working a desk job.

Workshop Objectives:

This training will help you:
• Learn why sedentary lifestyles are hazardous for your health
• Identify exercises to incorporate into your workday
• Understand the relationship between physical and mental stress
• Practice mind-body connection through stress management exercises

title: March 11 - Strategies for Validating World Models and Action-Conditioned Video
description:
Raleigh AI, Machine Learning and Computer Vision Meetup
Join us for a one hour hands-on workshop where we will explore emerging challenges in developing and validating world foundation models and video-generation AI systems for robotics and autonomous vehicles.

**Time and Location**

Mar 11, 2026
10-11am PST
Online, **[Register for the Zoom!](https://voxel51.com/events/debugging-the-future-strategies-for-validating-world-models-and-action-conditioned-video-march)**

Industries from robotics to autonomous vehicles are converging on world foundation models (WFMs) and action-conditioned video generation, where the challenge is predicting physics, causality, and intent. But this shift has created a massive new bottleneck: validation.

How do you debug a model that imagines the future? How do you curate petabyte-scale video datasets to capture the "long tail" of rare events without drowning in storage costs? And how do you ensure temporal consistency when your training data lives in scattered data lakes?

In this session, we explore technical workflows for the next generation of Visual AI. We will dissect the "Video Data Monster," demonstrating how to build feedback loops that bridge the gap between generative imagination and physical reality. Learn how leading teams are using federated data strategies and collaborative evaluation to turn video from a storage burden into a structured, queryable asset for embodied intelligence.

*About the Speaker*

[Nick Lotz](https://www.linkedin.com/in/nicholaslotz/) is chemical process engineer-turned-developer who is currently a Technical Marketing Engineer at Voxel51. He is particularly interested in bringing observability and security to all layers of the AI stack.
source: Meetup: Raleigh AI, ML & Computer Vision

title: Building Agent-Ready Data Architectures on Google Cloud
description:
Data Science Raleigh
AI agents are only as effective as the data they can access. Yet most enterprise architectures still depend on batch pipelines, fragmented systems, and tightly controlled operational environments that limit real-time context.

In this technical deep dive, Striim and Google Cloud outline a practical, production-ready architecture for streaming real-time operational data into Google Cloud to power AI at scale.
We’ll examine how low-latency Change Data Capture (CDC) enables continuous movement of trusted enterprise data from on-prem, hybrid, and multi-cloud environments into BigQuery and Cloud Storage—creating a dynamic foundation for analytics, model training, evaluation, and serving on Vertex AI.

The session goes beyond reference diagrams. We’ll explore how continuously refreshed data supports goal-directed reasoning in AI agents, improves memory retention through up-to-date context, and enables dynamic planning in real-world operational workflows. Just as importantly, we’ll address governance, observability, and architectural flexibility—so teams can modernize without compromising compliance or performance.

If you're building AI systems that need fresh enterprise context to act safely and effectively, this session will give you a blueprint grounded in real-world constraints.

**What You'll Learn:**

1️⃣ **Real-Time Data as Agent Context:** How low-latency CDC pipelines create continuously updated data products that power training, evaluation, and real-time inference on Vertex AI.

2️⃣ **Architecture for Hybrid and Multi-Cloud Enterprises:** Patterns for securely streaming operational data from on-prem and distributed systems into BigQuery and Cloud Storage without disrupting core systems.

3️⃣ **From Data Movement to AI Activation:** How fresh, governed data enables agent memory, dynamic decision-making, and modular AI workflows at scale.

4️⃣ **Governance, Observability, and Control:** How to maintain compliance, enforce access controls, and monitor data pipelines while supporting production-grade AI systems.

5️⃣**Future-Proof Platform Design:** How to architect for flexibility—so your AI stack can evolve as models, agents, and enterprise requirements change.

This is an architecture-led session for data and AI leaders who want to move beyond experimentation and build enterprise AI systems that operate in real time—securely, reliably, and at scale.

**[Register here](https://hubs.ly/Q043XKZQ0)**

title: Joint NC BERD Seminar: Statistical challenges in Long COVID research: Ongoing lessons from the RECOVER observational cohort
description:
Long COVID, also known as postacute sequelae of SARS-CoV-2 infection (PASC), is a chronic condition characterized by long-term symptoms that persist for months or even years after initial SARS-CoV-2 infection. Over 200 symptoms have been identified as being associated with Long COVID, affecting nearly every body system. Long COVID is known to manifest differently across individuals, wax and wane over time, and range from mild to incapacitating, with profound effects on quality of life. While recognition of Long COVID is growing among adults, it is still woefully understudied in children. The rapid materialization of large-scale observational data, including the Researching COVID to Enhance Recovery (RECOVER) meta-cohort, has generated enormous opportunity for novel discovery that could lead to effective prevention strategies and improved outcomes for patients living with Long COVID. The statistical challenges inherent in effectively and appropriately leveraging these novel data resources are numerous. In this talk, I will discuss my role as lead biostatistician of the Data Resource Core for RECOVER, present our work developing a working definition for a new chronic disease, and highlight the many statistical challenges in the study of Long COVID.

Zoom webinar link: https://duke.zoom.us/j/95276961581?pwd=y8FxiY7yBCweJ5zJddxMOzh7qikHTh.1

This event is being cross-promoted by the NC BERD Consortium, a collaboration of the CTSA-funded BERD cores at UNC-Chapel Hill, Wake Forest University School of Medicine, and Duke University School of Medicine.

title: Improve Psychological Resilience Counseling (Research Opportunity)
location: Prairie Ridge, 1671 Gold Star Drive, Raleigh, NC, 27607, United States
description:
Prairie Ridge is happy to host researcher Dr. Ashleigh Bruns for a special research opportunity! Dr. Bruns is working to learn how creative activities help build psychological resilience, an area of research she believes will help improve counseling and lead to reduced stress, anxiety, and depression in counseling patients. Her study involves taking a before and after survey and the artistic components are creating a haiku and an origami crane. Dr. Bruns will lead participants through how to do the activities – no experience necessary! Snacks will be provided.
Join us to do a few fun, creative tasks to help improve counseling opportunities for people in need!
More information about the study, including IRB approval information, is available here.
Register here

title: Phone Bank with Ground Truth
description:
Link to join: https://www.mobilize.us/join/47683001/1:998e5665400e92aaf4fcfa1f230105583e6d8000ae6b908bd4a99cce24f41ad1/

Join Swing Left for a Ground Truth phone bank—designed to fix what’s broken in Democratic organizing. We’re launching an innovative new program that will revolutionize how we connect with voters over the phone.

Our goal: to help Democrats rebuild trust, win tough races, and flip the House. We're bringing people together to build the community of real people, having real conversations with real people. During Ground Truth phone banks, we will talk to as many people as possible and focus on listening.

At this phone bank, we’ll:

- Call ALL types of voters -- not just those who vote like us.

- Have real conversations that LISTEN deeply to voters’ concerns.

- Gather insights to share with campaigns, so they can be responsive to voters’ needs and smarter with their messaging, targeting, and outreach.

Don’t worry if it’s your first time—training, materials, and support are all provided. Just come prepared with your phone and laptop or computer!

We recommend using earbuds or using speakerphone to free up your hands as well!

––

About the program:

This isn’t traditional phone banking—it’s a whole new way of talking to voters. Together, we’ll gather authentic insights from communities, use cutting-edge tools to capture what we hear, and share it back with campaigns to strengthen their efforts.

Why we’re starting early: We launched a year ahead of the midterms to build power before campaigns get off the ground.

How we’ll go big: Calling every voter, not just the ones we normally rely on, so every conversation builds a more complete picture of the electorate.

How we’ll go deep: Practicing empathetic listening and real conversations that uncover voters’ “Ground Truth,” not just checking boxes on a script.

Paid for by Swing Left, Swingleft.org, not authorized by any candidate or candidate’s committee.

How to prepare:
Thanks for signing up to join the Ground Truth phone banking program! Together, we can transform how we talk with voters, rebuild trust, and swing the most competitive races.

There will be a training at the beginning of the phone bank, so don't worry if this is your first time.

Please be sure to have your laptop/ computer charged and ready to go!

See you there!

https://www.mobilize.us/swingleft/event/872281/

title: Storytelling With Light
description: How does light influence how we feel? How can we use color and shape to showcase what is happening in the moment? title: Team ENOUGH Welcome Session (For Young People Under 26)
description:
Link to join: https://www.mobilize.us/join/46980069/1:3a66ca14e89d37ba12b3b113bf160b637af79ef4831d986f2bea0b1495822f76/

How to join:
Please join our virtual welcome session with this link

Are you a young person between 13 and 25 years old who is interested in taking action around preventing gun violence? Join us for our Team ENOUGH welcome session to learn more about the different ways you can engage.

Team ENOUGH is a youth-led program whose mission is to educate young voices about gun violence and mobilize them to take meaningful action against it. Supported by Brady United, we harness the expertise and experience of today’s youth to advance our intersectional work across the country.

Led by an Executive Council, comprised of students just like us, we believe that diversity is a strength, and that only when everyone has a seat at the table can we build an effective movement to prevent all gun violence in all communities.

For us, preventing gun violence not only requires regulating access to firearms but also addressing how systemic racism and environmental factors cause this form of bloodshed to run especially rampant in communities of color. You cannot talk about gun violence without talking about police brutality, white supremacy, the broken criminal justice system, poverty, homophobia, access to affordable housing, or the often barriers separating disadvantaged populations from physical and mental healthcare.

So if you share this belief, join us for our Team ENOUGH welcome session and become part of the movement to end gun violence in our lifetime.

How to prepare:
We are excited to see you soon!

P.S. This is a call for young people from 13-25 years old who are interested in getting engaged with the Team ENOUGH program. If you are over the age of 25 please sign up for the Brady Welcome Session at https://www.mobilize.us/brady/event/274016/

title: Monthly Welcome, Community, and Project Meeting
description:
Code with the Carolinas - Triangle
Code with the Carolinas is a community of people working together as civic tech volunteers to improve wellbeing in North and South Carolina, and we invite you to join us for our biweekly online meetings!
Our meetings start with introductions, updates on old and new business, and status reports on current projects, emphasizing how you can get involved. All are welcome and no technical skills are needed.

**Typical Agenda**
Introductions
Organizational Updates
Project updates

* Active projects like Zoning Atlas
* Proposal development for new projects

Meetings are on the Jitsi platform and the link is provided before each meeting. If you will be joining on mobile, install the Jitsi app in advance [https://jitsi.org/downloads/](https://jitsi.org/downloads/) If you joined on mobile and your audio isn't working, it might be because you joined through a browser and not through the app.

Learn more at [[codewiththecarolinas.org](http://codewiththecarolinas.org/)]([http://codeforthecarolinas.org](http://codeforthecarolinas.org/)), [join our Slack](https://join.slack.com/t/codewiththecarolinas/shared_invite/zt-3i6ptx0pz-raH_7Zx4sX0wFUkYM9w3Zw), or reach out to [info@codewiththecarolinas.org](http://info@codewiththecarolinas.org/)

All activities of Code with the Carolinas, a project of [Social Good Fund](https://www.socialgoodfund.org/), follow our [Code of Conduct](https://share.mailbox.org/ajax/share/0d32d5f905f8fd49d5e80395f8fd47f2b59ce3ef8b335f72/1/8/Nzg/NzgvMTkx) and are subject to this [Volunteer Waiver of Liability](https://share.mailbox.org/ajax/share/06dd62200e6b28426b137e0e6b2848fb901487014f72cd16/1/8/Nzg/NzgvMTE2).

title: Wednesday night meditation circle
description:
Durham Plum Village Meditation Circle
Join us for a meditation circle in the Plum Village tradition of Buddhist teacher Thich Nhat Hanh. We will practice a short seated meditation (10 minutes), walking meditation (10 minutes), dharma share on a topic of mindfulness by a fellow practitioner, group dharma sharing, and singing. We actively welcome and affirm BIPOC and queer people and seek to hold an inclusive space for all.

**Please note: Our location has changed to the Durham Friends Meetinghouse building in the Dogwood Room at 404 Alexander Ave, Durham, NC 27705**

The space has accessible parking, entrances, and gender neutral bathrooms. Chairs are available, and we encourage you to bring a cushion with you if you have one.

Our group is spirited, compassionate, and eager to learn mindfulness practices in community with one another. We have a suggested $0-5 donation to cover the cost of the space, with excess donations put toward group materials like spare cushions.

Please consider donating to our group on Venmo: [@DurhamPlumVillageMeditation](https://account.venmo.com/u/durhamplumvillagemeditation)

If you would like to be on our sangha email list, please reach out to durhamplumvillagemeditation@gmail.com

title: AI Hot Topics at NC State
location: Partners I – NC State University, Raleigh Founded (Suite 1650)
description:
The AI Hot Topics monthly networking series is a dynamic forum designed to foster dialogue and collaboration around the rapidly evolving artificial intelligence field. Whether you’re a seasoned AI practitioner, a curious scholar, or simply interested in the transformative potential of AI, this series offers a unique opportunity to connect with leading experts and contribute to meaningful discussions.

Date: March 12, 2026

Title: Beyond Prediction: Synthetic Data, Agentic Intelligence, and Quantum Acceleration.

Description: This session highlights three emerging AI trends shaping safer and more capable systems. It covers how synthetic data and digital twins enable secure, controlled experimentation; how agentic AI allows systems to observe, reason, and act within set boundaries; and when quantum-supported optimization can offer real benefits. Participants will gain a clear view of how these developments work together to support responsible, scalable decision-making across research and industry.

Moderator: Carlos Pinheiro,  Distinguished Data Scientist, SAS

Panelists:

Udo Sglavo, Vice President, Applied AI and Modeling R&D, SAS

Hardi Desai, Senior Data Scientist, Applied AI & Machine Learning, SAS

Munindar Singh, SAS Institute Distinguished Professor of Computer Science, NC State University

title: Harriet Jacobs in the Attic
description:
Join us for an immersive audiovisual experience as we illuminate Harriet Jacobs, a fugitive North Carolina enslaved person who hid in a tiny attic garret for seven years. While hiding she planned her escape, fled to the North, and eventually became a key figure in the abolitionist movement.

In celebration of Black History Month, we invite you to explore a to-scale recreation of Harriet’s attic space and listen to readings from her book, Incidents in the Life of a Slave Girl, which was published in 1861.

description: The Institute for Advanced Analytics' admissions team will provide an overview of the Master of Science in Analytics program and admissions process, then answer participants’ questions.

title: The Road Through “Don’t Ask, Don’t Tell”
description:
The Road Through Don’t Ask, Don’t Tell by photographer, oral historian, and U.S. Marine Corps veteran Theresa Scott. This deeply personal documentary exhibition explores the lived experiences of LGBTQIA+ veterans who served under restrictive military policies.
Through intimate portrait photography paired with first-person oral histories, Scott brings forward stories long silenced by institutional policy and cultural pressure. Drawing on her own service, she offers viewers an unflinching look at the realities faced by service members who were expected to serve with honor while hiding essential parts of who they were.
Exhibition Dates: February 1 – April 26, 2026
Artist Talk & Reception: March 26, 5:30–7:00 p.m.

title: Drawing and Understanding Conifer Trees in Micron Pen
location: North Carolina Botanical Garden, 100 Old Mason Farm Road, Chapel Hill, NC, 27517, United States
description:
Register Here

with Stacy-Ann Searle, Landscape Artist
Date: Thursday, March 12, 2026
Time: 9:30 a.m. – 3:30 p.m.
Location: North Carolina Botanical Garden
Fee: $78 ($70 Members)

Conifers come in many varieties. In this three-session workshop, we will explore how to capture the different overarching structure of these trees, as well as a variety of leaf/needle forms and bark textures. For this class, we will be using micron pens to create a variety of small ink studies. Students will gain an understanding of the different mark making techniques, how to create textures in ink and gain confidence in using this very approachable ink medium.

Register Here
