title: ERA - Guided Wildflower Hike - Penny's Bend
location: Penny's Bend, Old Oxford Rd, Durham, NC 27712, USA
description:
Registration is required - Tickets drop on February 1st at 8:00am!
Price: $4
*Ticket prices directly support the work of the Eno River Association. Thank you for your support!

This trail passes through open fields, deep forests, and rocky outcrops in less than two miles. Easy to moderate walking with one short, steep stretch that rewards with a bluff-top view of the river. Penny’s Bend is a horseshoe bend in the river formed by diabase rock. The underlying diabase here makes the soil basic, supporting plants unusual to the Piedmont. Spring wildflowers abound, including spectacular displays of Dutchman’s Breeches some years. If there have been recent rains, parts of the trail may be muddy and a walking stick is helpful. Link to a map of the trails at Penny’s Bend: https://www.alltrails.com/trail/us/north-carolina/pennys-bend

This hike will take approximately two hours (sometimes less, sometimes more). Please feel free to bring water and snacks. Wear comfortable, weather appropriate clothing and shoes. Well-behaved dogs are welcome as long as they are under your control and on a 6 foot leash.
This event is rain or shine. We will contact you if severe weather is expected that would cause us to cancel the hike.
If your plans change and you are unable to attend the hike, please contact education@enoriver.org to cancel your registration so that we may offer your spot(s) to our waitlist.

Directions: Coming from the west, take Duke Street North from I-85 (Exit 176). Turn right on Murray Avenue (sign to the Museum of Life and Science), go until it reaches Roxboro Street, turn left and look for the brown sign to Stagville. [Coming from the east, you can take Roxboro Street directly from I-85.] Turn right here on Old Oxford Road. Go about three miles until Old Oxford crosses the Eno. Immediately after the bridge, turn left on Snowhill Road. Penny’s Bend parking lot will be on the left immediately after you turn.

title: Phone Bank with Ground Truth
description:
Link to join: https://www.mobilize.us/join/47683004/1:af761e207e10ef33b75a0e76852bf654ae282bf5d5a310d4611f9ccf0a7e529a/

Join Swing Left for a Ground Truth phone bank—designed to fix what’s broken in Democratic organizing. We’re launching an innovative new program that will revolutionize how we connect with voters over the phone.

Our goal: to help Democrats rebuild trust, win tough races, and flip the House. We're bringing people together to build the community of real people, having real conversations with real people. During Ground Truth phone banks, we will talk to as many people as possible and focus on listening.

At this phone bank, we’ll:

- Call ALL types of voters -- not just those who vote like us.

- Have real conversations that LISTEN deeply to voters’ concerns.

- Gather insights to share with campaigns, so they can be responsive to voters’ needs and smarter with their messaging, targeting, and outreach.

Don’t worry if it’s your first time—training, materials, and support are all provided. Just come prepared with your phone and laptop or computer!

We recommend using earbuds or using speakerphone to free up your hands as well!

––

About the program:

This isn’t traditional phone banking—it’s a whole new way of talking to voters. Together, we’ll gather authentic insights from communities, use cutting-edge tools to capture what we hear, and share it back with campaigns to strengthen their efforts.

Why we’re starting early: We launched a year ahead of the midterms to build power before campaigns get off the ground.

How we’ll go big: Calling every voter, not just the ones we normally rely on, so every conversation builds a more complete picture of the electorate.

How we’ll go deep: Practicing empathetic listening and real conversations that uncover voters’ “Ground Truth,” not just checking boxes on a script.

Paid for by Swing Left, Swingleft.org, not authorized by any candidate or candidate’s committee.

How to prepare:
Thanks for signing up to join the Ground Truth phone banking program! Together, we can transform how we talk with voters, rebuild trust, and swing the most competitive races.

There will be a training at the beginning of the phone bank, so don't worry if this is your first time.

Please be sure to have your laptop/ computer charged and ready to go!

See you there!

title: Seasonal Wetland Ecology: Vernal Pools
description: Join a Ranger at the Horse Trail Parking Lot for a guided exploration of some of Stone Mountain State Park's vernal pools. Vernal pools are seasonal fresh-water wetland areas that are crucial for many amphibians like wood frogs, red-spotted newts, and yellow-spotted salamanders to lay their eggs. These pools dry up in late spring and summer and are a hotspot for ecologocial activity in early spring! Please wear close-toed shoes that are able to get wet, as well as appropriate clothing to wear off-trail through potentially thick vegetation. Additional materials will be provided.

title: Murmuration: Meet the Artists and Open Discussion
description:
Join us on Sunday, March 22, at 4:00 pm at Coker Arboretum to meet the artists of Murmuration, Jonathon Kirk and Lee Weisert, and participate in an open discussion about their process. Reserve your free tickets here: https://forms.gle/4rjnzPbwi4JkFgBa7

Next in “Rounding Home,” its eighteenth season, the Process Series is excited to present Murmuration, an outdoor soundscape installation by Jonathon Kirk and Lee Weisert.

The performance features one hundred wirelessly-controlled robotic woodblocks spread throughout the trees of Coker Arboretum, creating a soundscape that blurs the lines between the sounds of nature and music. The project was originally commissioned by the North Carolina Arts Council, receiving additional funding from New Music USA, and has recently been presented at the South Carolina Botanical Garden in Clemson and the State Arboretum of Virginia in Boyce, VA.

According to Weisert, “The piece explores the possibilities of physical scale and complexity in an artistic listening experience.” A wide variety of textures, waves, and gestures reminiscent of the natural sound sources they blend with—from chirping birds in the treetops to the crunching leaves beneath your feet—are generated in real time using tree-mounted woodblocks and computer programming. Describing the process behind the project, Weisert offers, “We are curious to discover to what extent music composition can ‘disappear’ into its surroundings. What role do proximity and distance play in our musical perception and interpretation? How closely can musical form and natural phenomena coexist?”

He adds, “The piece will hopefully act as a kind of sonic prism for rediscovering Coker Arboretum,” originally established in 1903, “which can be easily forgotten within the frenetic activity of campus life.”

Questions? Contact us at uncprocessseries@gmail.com or visit our website at https://www.processseries.unc.edu/current-season/murmuration

title: MFA 2026 Thesis Exhibitions at Lump Gallery
description:
Department of Art and Art History

Lump Presents: UNC-Chapel Hill MFA Thesis Exhibitions

March 6th - March 29th:

INFRA ULTRA by CJ Murphy / Under the Thumb by Michelle Lisa Polissaint

Opening reception: March 6, 6-9 pm

April 3rd - April 26th:

Nepantla by Wendy Yamilett Moran / Growing Elsewhere: Ghorbatī (غربتی) by Rojano Mohammadzadeh / Blood, Sap, Dirt by Iris Moyan Wang

Opening reception: April 3, 6-9 pm

Lump Gallery is located at 505 S. Blount St., Raleigh, NC 27601

Opening Hours: Saturdays and Sundays, 1-5 pm, and by appointment.

Lump Gallery is pleased to present thesis exhibitions by UNC-Chapel Hill’s 2026 MFA graduating class.

At the conclusion of a rigorous, research-based two-year program, these artists consider the concept of place and their personal relationships to it. Each artist employs a range of media and responds to geographic, constructed, technological, and bodily autonomy. These relationships between place and personal narrative raise broader questions around positionality and society; communication and identity; and human impact. Revelation, purpose, and possibility guide each artist’s research-centered practices.

CJ Murphy creates works that render the intersection of technology, magic, and globalization material by reinvigorating found and discarded electrical waste. Michelle Lisa Polissaint draws on her experience in a strict religious community and deep research into the BITE method to discuss contemporary issues of indoctrination, high-control groups, and authoritarianism. Rojano Mohammadzadeh draws on Islamic prayer, talismanic imagery, text, and ritualized gestures to approach the immigrant experience through what she terms transplantation: survival through negotiating acceptance within a host body. Wendy Yamilett Morán’s practice explores the intersections of culture, realism, and spirituality through painting and ceramics, using their work to examine how identity, memory, and belonging are shaped within in-between spaces. Iris Moyan Wang is an interdisciplinary artist working primarily with ink painting and ceramics to create fluid and erotic hybridizations of bodies and botanicals that reimagine Chinese cultural heritage.

For press inquiries or other questions, please contact uncmfathesis@yahoo.com

title: AI for Agents Workshop
description:
All Things AI (Live)
**Every AI agent is a delegation decision. This workshop teaches you how to make those decisions well.**

[To purchase tickets, visit [https://2026.allthingsai.org/register](https://2026.allthingsai.org/register) workshop tickets also include access to the All Things AI conference on March 24th. ***Price increases after Tuesday, March 17 so sign-up early and save $100*** ]

Many organizations have experimented with AI Agents. Far fewer have moved confidently into sustained, responsible execution.
As teams progress from early pilots to real-world deployment, leaders often encounter the same challenges: impressive prototypes that stall before production, uncertainty about where agents actually add value, late-breaking security and governance concerns, and misalignment between business and technical stakeholders about what “success” should look like. Too often, these issues surface only after cost, complexity, or risk have already compounded.
This workshop helps leaders make the shift from experimentation to execution—not by pushing agents everywhere, but by providing a clear blueprint for **when agents make sense, when they don’t, and how to make sound decisions when autonomy is on the table**.

***

#### **The Outcome: Better Decisions, Earlier**

By the end of this session, participants will have more than an overview of AI Agents. They will leave with a **practical decision-making framework** they can apply immediately to real initiatives.
You’ll be able to:

* determine whether an agent is appropriate for a given problem—or whether a copilot or traditional automation is the better choice
* evaluate agent proposals with a clear understanding of autonomy, risk, and tradeoffs
* anticipate the organizational, architectural, and governance factors that separate successful deployments from stalled experiments
* identify common failure modes early—while they are still inexpensive to address

The result is clearer judgment about **what to delegate to agents, what to constrain, and what should remain human**.

***

#### **What the Blueprint Covers**

In this intensive workshop, business and technical leaders will work through a practical blueprint for AI Agent success, grounded in real-world constraints and leadership realities.
Key topics include:

* understanding degrees of agency and how they change cost, risk, and accountability
* distinguishing agents from copilots and traditional automation—and why that distinction matters
* identifying use cases where agents consistently create value, and scenarios where they introduce unnecessary fragility
* recognizing common pitfalls that derail agent initiatives, from over-delegation to hidden operational complexity
* aligning organizational readiness, security, and governance with the level of autonomy being introduced

Participants will also receive a high-level technical orientation to the AI Agent ecosystem, including platforms, Model Context Protocol (MCP), and model selection tradeoffs. This includes a balanced discussion of the benefits and risks of using Small Language Models (SLMs) in agentic workflows, particularly when autonomy and tool access are involved.

***

#### **What You’ll Leave With**

Participants will leave with:

* A clear framework for deciding **if and when** AI Agents should be used
* A practical blueprint outlining the conditions required for agent success—and the signals that indicate trouble
* A realistic understanding of the cost, complexity, and operational demands of agent-based systems
* Clear guidance for incorporating security, governance, and human oversight from the start
* A shared language that aligns business and technical leaders around the same decision criteria

***

#### **Who This Is For**

This workshop is designed for business and technical leaders responsible for turning AI experimentation into operational reality, including:

* Leaders driving digital, AI, or transformation initiatives (e.g., Chief Digital Officers, Heads of AI, Transformation and Digital Strategy leaders)
* Technology and architecture leaders accountable for execution and scale (e.g., CTOs, CIOs, VPs of Engineering, Enterprise and Solution Architects)
* Product and innovation leaders shaping AI-enabled capabilities (e.g., CPOs, Heads of Product, Innovation and Emerging Technology leaders)
* Security, risk, and governance leaders involved in overseeing autonomous or AI-driven systems (e.g., CISOs, Responsible AI and Risk leaders)

This session is especially valuable for leaders who sit at the intersection of strategy and execution—those expected to decide **when AI Agents should be used, how much authority they should have, and how to govern them responsibly**.

[To purchase tickets, visit [https://2026.allthingsai.org/register](https://2026.allthingsai.org/register) workshop tickets also include access to the All Things AI conference on March 24th. ***Price increases after Tuesday, March 17 so sign-up early and save $100*** ]

AI isn’t coming. It’s already reshaping your industry.
But the advantage doesn’t go to the biggest company. It goes to the team that learns the fastest.
This course gives you that advantage.

title: All Things AI 2026
description:
All Things Open RTP Meetup
We invite you to join us at All Things AI 2026. We've partnered with [The Artificially Intelligent Enterprise (AIE) Network](https://theaie.net/) to bring you this event, and we're very excited! More than 1,600 from all over the world attended last March (2025), and it was a tremendous success.

**What is it?**
All Things AI is an AI practitioners and users conference and community focused on technologies, processes, and people. The event is designed to provide world-class educational and networking opportunities to attendees.

**[Website](https://2026.allthingsai.org/)**

**Format:**
**Monday, March 23**
Training day with multiple paid workshops being hosted: **AI for DevOps**, **AIOS Workshop For Business Professionals**, and **AI for Agents**. A number of free workshops will be added soon, to include Major League Hacking's AI Roadshow.
***\*note, separate registration is required for paid and free workshops/sessions.***
***\*\*A paid workshop registration on Monday DOES include Tuesday registration.***

**Tuesday, March 24**
Traditional conference day with morning keynotes, lunchtime AI Deep Thought (Lightning) Talks, and 30-minute sessions across 7 tracks: **AI Builders**, **AI Engineers**, **AI Users**, **AI Executives**, **AI Governance**, **Security & Compliance**, **AI in the Enterprise (Transformation & Infrastructure)**, and **Case Study / Demo**.
***\*note, separate registration is required for Tuesday***

[See v 1.0 of the schedule here](https://2026.allthingsai.org/schedule)

**Speakers:**
Includes some of the most well-known AI experts in the world from companies like AWS, IBM, Meta, GitHub, Block, Fidelity Investments, Truist, AuthO/Okta, Netflix, Visa, Apple, Intuit, Mistral AI, and more.
[See all speakers here](https://2026.allthingsai.org/speakers)

**Food & Beverages:**
Lunch (boxed lunch and drink) is included with paid registration for Monday and Tuesday. Multiple restaurants are available and easily accessible in downtown Durham around the conference venue.

**Registration:**
We've worked hard to keep registration costs low and the event accessible. [Check out the registration page here](https://2026.allthingsai.org/register).

**Scholarships & Discounts:**
We understand personal situations and ability to pay varies, so we've worked with partners to make a variety of scholarships and discounts available. [See what's available here](https://2026.allthingsai.org/scholarships-discounts).

Questions? Contact us at **info@allthingsopen.org**.

description:

As part of the 16 Days of Activism Against Gender-Based Violence campaign, Right To Be in partnership with the NYC Commission on Gender Equity (CGE) and the Mayor’s Office to End Domestic and Gender-Based Violence (ENDGBV), will host an online virtual bystander intervention training to equip New Yorkers with tools to stand up against harassment. The 16 Days campaign is a global initiative that runs from November 25 (International Day for the Elimination of Violence Against Women) through December 10 (Human Rights Day), raising awareness to prevent and end gender-based violence. The training focuses on encouraging community action, offering practical strategies skills to safely intervene when witnessing help someone who is experiencing hate-based harassment, and join reinforcing the city’s commitment effort to build fostering safer, more equitable spaces for all.

title: The Morbid Zoo: Reevaluating Academia for the Internet Age
description: In 2019, YouTube creator Mariana Colín started building her film analysis YouTube channel, The Morbid Zoo, as an exercise in developing the video essay format as a legitimate mode of academic publishing. Since then, her videos have been viewed over 9 million times. YouTube is accessible and customizable, offering a way to engage directly with a nonacademic audience. However, it is also isolating, biased toward particular kinds of engagement; it is also deeply politically fraught. In this talk, Colín will share what she’s discovered about social media’s academic use — its successes, its failures and its possible futures.

title: The Art and Science of Plant Care
description:
Caring for plants is both an art and a science — part intuition, part know-how. Every gardener, from beginner to expert, faces the same essential questions: When should I prune? How much should I water? What’s the best way to plant, fertilize or repot? In this class, Bryce Lane will bring decades of horticultural expertise to demystify these everyday tasks, showing you not just what to do but why it works.

Over eight weeks, you’ll explore the fundamental practices that keep gardens thriving, learning how to perform each one with confidence and purpose. You’ll uncover the science behind good technique — understanding how soil, water, nutrients and plant biology interact — while also developing the practical judgment that comes only with experience.

Each session will focus on one key aspect of plant care, building a complete toolkit for growing healthier, more resilient plants in any garden setting.

title: Civic Hacking for Affordable Housing
description:
Code with the Carolinas - Triangle
Join volunteers at Code with the Carolinas as we help the [National Zoning Atlas](https://www.zoningatlas.org/) (NZA) team include North Carolina and South Carolina in their nationwide zoning atlas. "The National Zoning Atlas aims to depict key aspects of zoning codes in an online, user-friendly map." The Atlas aims to provide data for better solutions in transportation, environmental, and especially affordable housing policy.

Affordable housing proponents have long recognized zoning policies as barriers to affordable housing. To improve zoning policies, we first need to better understand the current state of zoning across the United States. Code with the Carolinas is helping this cause by joining the National Zoning Atlas to gather zoning data for North and South Carolina.

This is a multi-dimensional project grounded in data science with an end-goal of contributing to a nationally influential dataset. Our current work session activities focus on internet search and integrating data from multiple sources. The project as a whole has occasional opportunities for U/X and outreach and opportunities to learn about machine learning and natural language processing through the work of the NZA team. Volunteering on this project will provide excellent exposure to the foundations of civic data science, with a focus on GIS data.

Even if you're new to this project and/or civic tech volunteering, you're welcome to join the meeting. Visitors are always welcome and no technical skills are needed. You can learn more about the project on our [Slack Workspace](https://join.slack.com/t/codeforthecarolinas/shared_invite/zt-1kxuwu05x-3KxOpkOYjAuN5yuOAH8ROg) in the #project-zoning-atlas channel.

Meetings start with brief introductions. New volunteers are offered a personal orientation, often in a breakout room, while experienced volunteers get right to work. By the end of the session, both groups of volunteers are working together. We also contribute to the project through asynchronous work and informal work sessions.

Meetings are on the Jitsi platform and the link is provided before each meeting. If you will be joining on mobile, install the Jitsi app in advance [https://jitsi.org/downloads/](https://jitsi.org/downloads/) .

Learn more at [codewiththecarolinas.org](codeforthecarolinas.org) or reach out to info@codewiththecarolinas.org

All activities of Code with the Carolinas, a project of [Social Good Fund](https://www.socialgoodfund.org/), follow our [Code of Conduct](https://share.mailbox.org/ajax/share/0d32d5f905f8fd49d5e80395f8fd47f2b59ce3ef8b335f72/1/8/Nzg/NzgvMTkx) and are subject to this [Volunteer Waiver of Liability](https://share.mailbox.org/ajax/share/06dd62200e6b28426b137e0e6b2848fb901487014f72cd16/1/8/Nzg/NzgvMTE2).

title: The Triangle: Best Place to Live in the U.S.?
description:
This Community Classroom event will be held on March 24, 2026; Registration is required to attend.

Why are so many people moving to the Triangle, and what does this growth mean for current residents?

Across the country, rising housing costs and declining economic security are reshaping where people can afford to live. In places like California, homeownership is out of reach for educated, middle-class families.

The Raleigh–Durham–Chapel Hill region is now one of the nation’s fastest-growing destinations for people who are leaving their current homes in search of affordability, opportunity, and a better quality of life. This Community Classroom presentation explores why the Triangle attracts newcomers, and what that rapid growth means for housing, consumer costs, environmental sustainability, and a shared sense of place for those who already live here.

Drawing on research and lived experience, this free presentation examines multiple sides of this story. It considers the benefits migrants find here, the concerns they bring, and increasingly heated debates over development and growth. As newcomers engage in local politics, the region faces critical questions about current and future population expansion.

Can the Triangle grow without repeating the inequalities and environmental challenges our new neighbors hoped to escape? This conversation on Tuesday, March 24, 2026 at 6:30 PM invites reflection on how today’s choices will shape the region for generations.

About the Presenter

Nina Martin, PhD, is an urban geographer focused on how cities grow, change, and address inequality. Her research links urban planning and public policy with housing, economic development, and labor markets. She is particularly interested in how “progressive” cities can unintentionally reproduce the very inequities they aim to solve.

She teaches urban geography and globalization courses at UNC–Chapel Hill. She also serves as Director of Research Curricula in the Office of Undergraduate Research, supporting interdisciplinary research by students and faculty. Additionally, Dr. Martin serves on the Durham Workers’ Rights Commission, advising the City Council on issues affecting working people.

Dr. Martin earned a PhD from the University of Illinois at Chicago. She holds an MSc from the London School of Economics, and received her BA from McGill University in Montreal.

title: Staying Alive
location: North Carolina Museum of Natural Sciences
description:
March 14 – August 23, 2026

In the ultimate battle for survival, every animal has a plan.

Explore the striking ways nature fights back in “Staying Alive,” featuring 17 live animals from around the world with unbelievable self-defense strategies. Come face to face with trippy toads, armored armadillos, a two-toed sloth and more.

Discover how these defenders deploy an amazing arsenal of high-fashion camouflage and mimicry, specialized armor and advanced chemical warfare – like stink bombs, caboose cannons and smoke screens – to hide in plain sight, stand their ground or even drop a limb and make a run for it. Enjoy live animal encounters, larger-than-life sculptures, 3D tactile experiences and interactive programs.

Perfect for families and animal enthusiasts of all ages. Offered in English & Spanish.

title: Right to Be's How To Respond 2.0: Practical Tools for Being Powerful in the Face of Harassment
description:
REGISTER HERE: https://righttobe.org/events/how-to-respond-2-0-practical-tools-for-being-powerful-in-the-face-of-harassment-6/

Building on Right To Be’s How to Respond to Harassment training and the methodology of Self Offense, this workshop dives deeply into the art of “reclaiming your space,” Right To Be’s supportive skill for confidently responding to harassment. In today’s world, where personal boundaries often feel increasingly challenged, the power to respond confidently to harassment has never been more vital. We all deserve to feel safe and confident in our spaces, but may hesitate to respond in ways that honor both our instinctive reactions and our desire to reclaim our power in these situations.
Pulling from Self Offense’s somatic and embodied expertise – and 10 degrees of black belt between them – this training equips participants with:
Simple tools to assess situations objectively
Practical strategies to harness your natural responses
Concrete ways to set boundaries and reclaim your space
Real-world techniques for building lasting resilience
Through guided practice and supportive discussion, participants will gain practical experience in turning reactive moments into opportunities for empowered action, develop renewed confidence in their instincts, and learn to respond powerfully to harassment while staying true to themselves.

title: Goldston America 250 Film Series
description:
Join the Chatham Community Library for this Goldston America 250 Film Series, hosted every fourth Tuesday, January-December 2026, 4pm-6pm. America 250’s mission is to honor and commemorate the 250th anniversary of the signing of the Declaration of Independence, marking America’s Semiquincentennial. The Goldston Library will screen the following documentaries and films:

Tue, Jan 27, 4pm-6pm The Congress, Ken Burns.Tue, Feb 24, 4pm-6pm Becoming Frederick Douglass, Stanley Nelson and Nicole London.Tue, Mar 24, 4pm-6pm Rachel Carson: The Woman Who Launched the Modern Environmental Movement, Michelle Ferrari.Tue, Apr 28, 4pm-6pm Thomas Jefferson: A View from the Mountain, Martin Doblmeier.Tue, May 26, 4pm-6pm The Address, Ken Burns.Tue, Jun 23, 4pm-6pm The Brooklyn Bridge, Ken Burns.Tue, Jul 28, 4pm-6pm The Statue of Liberty, Ken Burns.Tue, Aug 25, 4pm-6pm American Coup: Wilmington 1898, Yoruba Richen and Brad Lichtenstein.Tue, Sep 22, 4pm-6pm How it Feels to be Free, Yoruba Richen.Tue, Oct 27, 4pm-6pm 80 Years Later, Celine Parreñas Shimizu.Tue, Nov 24, 4pm-6pm Gather, Sanjay Rawal.Tue, Dec 22, 4pm-6pm Women of World War II: The Untold Stories, Eli Brown.This FREE, family-friendly event is open to the public. No registration is required. The event will take place indoors in the Goldston Library. The Goldston Library is ADA-compliant, including wheelchair-accessible parking, entrances, seating, common spaces, walkways, and restrooms. Free parking is available outside the library. Pets are not allowed - please leave your furry friends at home.

This event is part of our Chatham Celebrates America 250 initiative. See more America 250 events.

title: *IN-PERSON* SF/F Group
description:
Durham Writers Group
We will be at the People's Solidarity Hub at 1809 Chapel Hill Rd, Durham. This venue does not serve food or drink, but feel free to bring your own. There is a kitchenette for our use, but be sure to clean up afterwards. The space might be a little cozy; please consider masking or staying home if you have a respiratory ailment.

This sub-group focuses on Speculative Fiction, including Science Fiction, Fantasy and Horror. If you have anything that falls under that category — be it short story, poem, novel-in-progress or screenplay — then come! We'd love to hear from you.

Our format allows each reader 15 minutes to present their work aloud, followed by approximately 20 minutes of feedback from the other attendees. To ensure fairness, each attendee is limited to 1 minute of Initial Feedback time. During that period, the author remains silent. Next, we have a 5 minute Free-For-All discussion about the work -- then the author may speak. (We usually get through three readers.)

Though not required, in order to receive more in-depth feed-back, readers may post their work in advance on our Google Group:
https://groups.google.com/d/forum/durham-sff-writers-group

We ask that participants keep their comments constructive and respectful, focusing on what works or doesn't work in the piece.

We also ask that readers provide a warning, both on the Google Group and during the meeting, if their story features content that some readers might find upsetting. This includes descriptions of violence or sexual content.

Readers are:
•
•
•

Backup Readers are:
•
•
•

Deep Backup:
•

***

***We meet every Tuesday at the same time, but alternate between In-Person*** ***and on Zoom. Members may attend both formats, if desired, but readers will be listed separately under the In-Person or Zoom weeks.***

title: Global Film Series: Okko’s Inn
location: James B. Hunt Jr. Library, Auditorium
description:
The Global Film Series will present a screening of Okko’s Inn.

After losing her parents in a car accident, Okko goes to live in the countryside with her grandmother, who runs a traditional Japanese inn built on top of an ancient spring said to have healing waters. While she goes about her chores and prepares to become the inn’s next caretaker, Okko discovers some spirits live there that only she can see — not scary ones but welcoming ghosts who keep her company, play games and help her navigate her new environment. The inn’s motto is that it welcomes all and will reject none, and this is soon put to the test as a string of new guests challenge Okko’s ability to be a gracious host. But ultimately, Okko discovers that dedicating herself to others becomes the key to taking care of herself.

The film's runtime is 1 hour, 34 minutes. The movie is in Japanese with English subtitles.

The Global Film Series is a collaboration between the NC State University Libraries and NC State’s Office of Global Engagement. This screening is in partnership with NC State’s Women’s Center in celebration of Women’s History Month.

title: Voices Rising: Save Our Sounds (Black Mountain)
description:
Join NCWF Swannanoa Valley WILD! at Highland Farms in Black Mountain for a conversation about one of North Carolina’s most treasured natural resources: our estuarine sounds. Estuaries, sometimes described as “nurseries of the sea”, are crucial because they filter pollutants, provide critical nurseries for fish and shellfish, as well as economic benefits through recreation, tourism, and jobs, all while supporting immense biodiversity. Our shallow sounds are in deep trouble for several reasons, not the least of which is our fisheries management. It’s not too late to recognize and to cherish North Carolina’s natural treasures for the gift they are and undertake-with dogged determination-the effort to restore their abundance, for humans and the environment. Restoration is not a pipe dream; it is just waiting for us to get on board.
Our speaker, Ann Berry Somers, is a passionate conservationist and a Senior Lecturer Emerita at the University of North Carolina at Greensboro. Somers serves on the Board of Directors for the N.C. Wildlife Federation.
REGISTER HERE

title: Travel CHINA like a local! Virtual QnA [x-posted]
description:
Raleigh & Triangle Activities Meetup Group
Putting out feelers for curious, cooperative 'comrades' to explore quiet corners of Old Cathay! Trip date TBD - waiting for airfares to come down.

This will happen on Google Meet.

**CHECK THE COMMENTS FOR MEETING LINK!**

[Comment if you are able to log onto the link but don't see me - in the past Google has let two meetings with the same link open, each person thinking the other didn't show up. So comment here and we'll see if we can fix it. :-) ]

I've been traveling to China for 27 years. Join me for a unique, off the beaten track culture immersion adventure!

Comfort NOT guaranteed! LOTS of walking! This trip is NOT for everyone but everyone who has come with me so far has LOVED it!

Itinerary NOT guaranteed so as to maximize experience! Spontaneous private zheng concert in a riverside pavilion? Let's stop and enjoy! Weather is forecast to be rain in the mountains? Let's go to the ancient village instead.

Costs currently unknown but should be under $100/day for ALL in-country necessities: food, lodging, entrance fees, ground transport. Whatever price I end up quoting will NOT include transport to/from China, alcohol, upgrades to lodging\*, upgrades to in-country transport\*.

You can do it cheaper with a big box tour. If strict time schedules, overpriced shopping, industrial restaurant food, and being kept away from locals is good enough for you than that's your best bet. If unique, real China, great food, insider knowledge with eye for western comforts\*\*, and turn on a dime\*\*\* [a mao?] escapades are for you than come with me!

Only seven people! Small and 'nimble'\*\*\*!

Have you already been to China? Already have seen the Forbidden City and Great Wall? No worries! Big box tours do NOT go where I go!

Have you never been to China and NEED to see the big sights? No problem! I can create an optional 3 day add-on for the Great Wall, Forbidden City, Temple of Heaven, and other Beijing highlights. AND I can take you to hidden gems few big tours touch.

Let's dialog! Looking for like minded people so everyone's on the same page. Don't want to put hard-core shoppers with shop-hating history buffs, or long distance hikers with casual strollers. Just a more practical way to go. If enough interest I can lead TWO trips, each with its own emphasis. I reserve the right to group/accept/deny as I see fit. [I only want happiness! Sometimes that means saying 'maybe next time'.]

Go to Jan Tu Tours on FB and browse the photo albums to get a hint of what one might see. No guarantees of ANYTHING! China changes very quickly.

\*Where available - LOTS of things NOT available off the beaten track. I said 'adventure'!

\*\*Sometimes western comforts are just not there. Deep in the countryside villages rarely have western toilets.

\*\*\*Quick change of plans is relative in China; western expectations are best set aside and an embrace the new/unknown/surprise attitude is highly recommended. But that's what a cultural adventure is all about!

title: Ackland Film Forum: "Crooklyn" (Spike Lee, 1994)
location: Varsity Theatre, 123 E. Franklin Street, Chapel Hill, NC, 27514, United States
description:
Set in 1970s Brooklyn, Crooklyn recounts childhood from the perspective of nine-year-old Troy Carmichael (Zelda Harris), whose coming of age unfolds within the rhythms of Black family life, neighborhood culture, and social constraint. The film balances warmth and humor with moments of rupture, revealing how maturity often arrives through confrontation with authority, injustice, and grief. Lee presents growing up as both deeply personal and inseparable from place, race, and community—an education shaped as much by environment as by experience.

The film will be introduced by Daelena Tinnin-Gadson, Assistant Professor of Black Film Studies and African American Literature.
This film will be screened at 7:30 p.m. at the Varsity Theatre (123 East Franklin Street, Chapel Hill, NC 27514). Tickets are free, but must be reserved on the Ackland’s webpage. Register below. UNC-Chapel Hill students can receive CLE credit for attending by scanning the QR code at the event.

About the Series:
Join the Ackland Film Forum this spring for Growing Up, Looking Back, a series that accompanies the Ackland exhibition Bill Bamberger: Boys Will Be Men. This semester’s series highlights the filmgoing experience by using three local movie theaters as its venues.
This film series is dedicated to the memory of Allison Portnow Lathrop, former Head of Public Programs at the Ackland Art Museum. For more than a decade, Allison was an indispensable champion of film culture on campus and in Chapel Hill, most notably through her visionary stewardship of the Ackland Film Forum. She fostered a spirit of generous collaboration among faculty, students, and artists. Her belief in the power of cinema lives on in the conversations and shared experiences she helped create.
The Ackland Film Forum is co-organized by the Ackland Art Museum and UNC Film Studies, housed in the UNC-Chapel Hill Department of English and Comparative Literature.
source: Ackland Art Museum, UNC Chapel Hill

title: Staying Alive
location: North Carolina Museum of Natural Sciences
description:
March 14 – August 23, 2026

In the ultimate battle for survival, every animal has a plan.

Explore the striking ways nature fights back in “Staying Alive,” featuring 17 live animals from around the world with unbelievable self-defense strategies. Come face to face with trippy toads, armored armadillos, a two-toed sloth and more.

Discover how these defenders deploy an amazing arsenal of high-fashion camouflage and mimicry, specialized armor and advanced chemical warfare – like stink bombs, caboose cannons and smoke screens – to hide in plain sight, stand their ground or even drop a limb and make a run for it. Enjoy live animal encounters, larger-than-life sculptures, 3D tactile experiences and interactive programs.

Perfect for families and animal enthusiasts of all ages. Offered in English & Spanish.

title: Avoiding Burnout: Self-Assessment Methods and Strategies for Self-Care
description:
Workplace burnout is at an all-time high, with one recent study reporting more than 42% of the workforce to be experiencing components of burnout. This session will dive in the components of burnout, create reflection opportunities for participants to assess their burnout levels, and provide helpful options for pro-active self-care. Participants will reflect on their “why” or their inner motivation for doing the work they are doing and how to better connect their why to their everyday work to become happier and healthier versions of themselves.

Workshop Objectives
This training will help you:
• Describe factors that increase the likelihood that burnout may become a problem
• Identify signals that indicate burnout is beginning
• Reflect on their inner motivation for completing their role
• Initiate a new self-care plan that includes healthy boundaries

Click on Carolina Talent registration link and select the “Request” button to automatically enroll.

For more information about our club and how being a member can benefit you, please contact our VP of Public Relations, Keisha Demps. Be sure to check out the Toastmasters International website, too.

title: Lunchtime Discovery: No Girls Allowed? Breaking Barriers for Women in Geology
location: Nature Research Center, 121 West Jones Street, Raleigh, NC, 27601, United States
description:
Join us in-person in the SECU Daily Planet Theater or watch the livestream on YouTube.
The geology profession has been, and continues to be, a field dominated by men. At 18 years old, when Amy decided to be a geologist, she had no idea. To her, geology meant that she got to outside, get dirty, and study the earth – being a woman never factored into her career decision. Over the past 25 years, there have been professional highs and lows, obstacles to overcome, and barriers to be broken. All of this helped Amy to rediscover her voice and end up with the best job a geologist could imagine. Join Amy for a look back on her geology journey.
Amy Pitts, Senior Geologist for Education and Outreach, North Carolina Geological Survey, North Carolina Department of Environmental Quality

Throughout the month of March, the Lunchtime Discovery Series will recognize Women’s History Month and women’s contributions to science and conservation. These live virtual presentations will be held each Wednesday from noon to 1:00 p.m. and are hosted by the NC Museum of Natural Sciences Coordinator of Current Science Programs Chris Smith. Brought to you by the NC DEQ Office of Environmental Education and Public Affairs and the NC Museum of Natural Sciences.

title: Building with Mistral: Open, Powerful, and Ready to Scale
description:
Data Science Raleigh
Explore how to develop and deploy Enterprise AI applications with Mistral AI in this practical, insight-driven session. We’ll walk through Mistral’s full model spectrum—from Large to Small—and how to select the right capabilities for your specific use case.

You’ll get an introduction to Mistral AI Studio and learn how to begin building, customizing, and managing AI applications with speed and control. The session will also cover flexible deployment pathways, including serverless, cloud, on-premise, Cloud-VPC, and dedicated instances—so you can align infrastructure with enterprise requirements.

**What You’ll Learn:**

🔹 **Model Selection Strategy:** Understand the Large-to-Small model spectrum and when to use each.
🔹 **Application Development:** Build and tailor AI applications in Mistral AI Studio.
🔹 **Flexible Deployment Options:** Compare serverless, cloud, on-prem, VPC, and dedicated setups.
🔹 **Production Best Practices:** Apply practical guidance to move AI projects into enterprise-scale production.

Gain the clarity and tools needed to take AI initiatives from experimentation to reliable, production-ready systems.

**[Register here](https://hubs.ly/Q045pnzM0)**

title: Right to Be's Bystander Intervention to Support Trans* Equality
description:
REGISTER HERE: https://righttobe.org/events/bystander-intervention-to-support-trans-equality-8/

This one-hour, interactive training will teach you Right To Be’s 5Ds of bystander intervention methodology. We’ll start by talking about identities that fall under the trans* umbrella and the types of disrespect that trans* folks are facing right now — from microaggressions to violence — using a tool we call the “spectrum of disrespect.” You’ll also learn a little bit about the impacts of disrespect and harassment on trans* identifying folks and the positive impact that intervention can have. We’ll talk through five strategies for intervention: distract, delegate, document, delay, and direct; and how to prioritize your own safety while intervening.
Finally, we’ll practice intervening with a series of scenarios and close with a brief visioning exercise, so you leave feeling more confident intervening the next time you see transphobic harassment online or in person.

title: Build a Website! Step-by-Step Workshop
description:
On March 25th at 4:30PM in Chapman Hall Room 201, come attend a fully guided, personal website-building tutorial hosted by the Sports Analytics Intelligence Lab (SAIL). This event is open to all members of the UNC community, but will be most beneficial to those who currently do not have a personal website. By the end of this tutorial, you will have all the tools to replace that boring, paper resume with a professional looking website that was created using the R programming language and is hosted for free on Github.com.

UNC Alum and current graduate student, Abigail Mabe will be leading this tutorial. Abigail was one of the founding members of SAIL and is currently a graduate advisor for SAIL. If you would like to participate in this event, please go to https://aemabe.github.io/Getting-Started-with-R-and-GitHub/ and follow the steps. This will ensure that you have all of the software needed to jump into the live tutorial on March 25th.

Undergraduate students will receive a CLE credit for their participation in this event.

title: Phone Bank with Ground Truth
description:
Link to join: https://www.mobilize.us/join/47683005/1:7314678383ddf9885da04618ba716a33b4425b135ab6daf9dd79edc74b87de64/

Join Swing Left for a Ground Truth phone bank—designed to fix what’s broken in Democratic organizing. We’re launching an innovative new program that will revolutionize how we connect with voters over the phone.

Our goal: to help Democrats rebuild trust, win tough races, and flip the House. We're bringing people together to build the community of real people, having real conversations with real people. During Ground Truth phone banks, we will talk to as many people as possible and focus on listening.

At this phone bank, we’ll:

- Call ALL types of voters -- not just those who vote like us.

- Have real conversations that LISTEN deeply to voters’ concerns.

- Gather insights to share with campaigns, so they can be responsive to voters’ needs and smarter with their messaging, targeting, and outreach.

Don’t worry if it’s your first time—training, materials, and support are all provided. Just come prepared with your phone and laptop or computer!

We recommend using earbuds or using speakerphone to free up your hands as well!

––

About the program:

This isn’t traditional phone banking—it’s a whole new way of talking to voters. Together, we’ll gather authentic insights from communities, use cutting-edge tools to capture what we hear, and share it back with campaigns to strengthen their efforts.

Why we’re starting early: We launched a year ahead of the midterms to build power before campaigns get off the ground.

How we’ll go big: Calling every voter, not just the ones we normally rely on, so every conversation builds a more complete picture of the electorate.

How we’ll go deep: Practicing empathetic listening and real conversations that uncover voters’ “Ground Truth,” not just checking boxes on a script.

Paid for by Swing Left, Swingleft.org, not authorized by any candidate or candidate’s committee.

How to prepare:
Thanks for signing up to join the Ground Truth phone banking program! Together, we can transform how we talk with voters, rebuild trust, and swing the most competitive races.

There will be a training at the beginning of the phone bank, so don't worry if this is your first time.

Please be sure to have your laptop/ computer charged and ready to go!

See you there!

https://www.mobilize.us/swingleft/event/872281/

title: Team ENOUGH Welcome Session (For Young People Under 26)
description:
Link to join: https://www.mobilize.us/join/46980071/1:7bb167af3389aded56f7394d46fb0733ac3056cd655df24c5826cb1a90ef4721/

How to join:
Please join our virtual welcome session with this link

Are you a young person between 13 and 25 years old who is interested in taking action around preventing gun violence? Join us for our Team ENOUGH welcome session to learn more about the different ways you can engage.

Team ENOUGH is a youth-led program whose mission is to educate young voices about gun violence and mobilize them to take meaningful action against it. Supported by Brady United, we harness the expertise and experience of today’s youth to advance our intersectional work across the country.

Led by an Executive Council, comprised of students just like us, we believe that diversity is a strength, and that only when everyone has a seat at the table can we build an effective movement to prevent all gun violence in all communities.

For us, preventing gun violence not only requires regulating access to firearms but also addressing how systemic racism and environmental factors cause this form of bloodshed to run especially rampant in communities of color. You cannot talk about gun violence without talking about police brutality, white supremacy, the broken criminal justice system, poverty, homophobia, access to affordable housing, or the often barriers separating disadvantaged populations from physical and mental healthcare.

So if you share this belief, join us for our Team ENOUGH welcome session and become part of the movement to end gun violence in our lifetime.

How to prepare:
We are excited to see you soon!

P.S. This is a call for young people from 13-25 years old who are interested in getting engaged with the Team ENOUGH program. If you are over the age of 25 please sign up for the Brady Welcome Session at https://www.mobilize.us/brady/event/274016/

title: Dr. M.T. Pope House Presents: Evelyn's Book Club
description:
This program honors Evelyn Pope by reading books.

Evelyn, a college librarian born in 1908 and died in 1995, loved a variety of books from The Adventures of Tom Sawyer to Shakespeare and James Baldwin. The program brings community members together monthly at the museum to discuss books, focusing on works by Black writers or featuring Black subjects, including fiction and non-fiction. Discussions will use questions to facilitate conversation.

"Mama Day" by Gloria Naylor
Wednesday, Feb. 25
"The Black Angels: The Untold Story of the Nurses Who Helped Cure Tuberculosis" by Maria Smilios
Wednesday, April 22
"A Visitation of Spirits" by Randall Kenan
Wednesday, June 24
"Becoming Lunsford Lane: The Lives of an American Aeneas" by Craig Friend
Wednesday, Aug. 26
"Ring Shout" by P. Dejeli Clark
Thursday, Oct. 29Registration opens Jan. 23.  An optional book bundle will be available to purchase at registration. Books can be picked up at the Dr. M.T. Pope House Museum.

title: Wednesday night meditation circle
description:
Durham Plum Village Meditation Circle
Join us for a meditation circle in the Plum Village tradition of Buddhist teacher Thich Nhat Hanh. We will practice a short seated meditation (10 minutes), walking meditation (10 minutes), dharma share on a topic of mindfulness by a fellow practitioner, group dharma sharing, and singing. We actively welcome and affirm BIPOC and queer people and seek to hold an inclusive space for all.

**Please note: Our location has changed to the Durham Friends Meetinghouse building in the Dogwood Room at 404 Alexander Ave, Durham, NC 27705**

The space has accessible parking, entrances, and gender neutral bathrooms. Chairs are available, and we encourage you to bring a cushion with you if you have one.

Our group is spirited, compassionate, and eager to learn mindfulness practices in community with one another. We have a suggested $0-5 donation to cover the cost of the space, with excess donations put toward group materials like spare cushions.

Please consider donating to our group on Venmo: [@DurhamPlumVillageMeditation](https://account.venmo.com/u/durhamplumvillagemeditation)

If you would like to be on our sangha email list, please reach out to durhamplumvillagemeditation@gmail.com

title: Duke Arts Presents: TESTO by Wet Mess
description:
Testo is an extraordinary, intimate, and unflinchingly raw solo show from Wet Mess-one of the most electrifying performers on the UK's drag and theatre circuits. Blending verbatim testimony, lip-sync, dance, and disorienting cabaret, Wet Mess uses their formidable stagecraft to explore transmasculinity, bodily autonomy, queer identity, and the spaces in-between. Winner of Not Another Drag Competition at London's legendary Royal Vauxhall Tavern, Mess delivers a performance that is at once grotesque and gorgeous-a visceral unraveling of binaries and a celebration of becoming.

Recommended for audiences age 18+ due to nudity and mature content.

title: The First Oval Office Project
description:
Experience George Washington’s Mobile Headquarters during the American Revolution!

The Museum of the American Revolution’s First Oval Office Project is coming to Tryon Palace and will be open for tours from March 26, through 28.

The First Oval Office Project is an inspiring handsewn, reproduction of General George Washington’s mobile headquarters that travelled with him throughout the American Revolution.  The reproduction encampment includes the sleeping and office marquee, dining marquee, baggage tent, camp bed, furniture, and more that were part of his mobile headquarters while commanding the Continental Army, as well as other tents for common soldiers in the Continental Army.  A team of interpreters from the Museum of the American Revolution will be on site to guide you and answer questions about his military life while on campaign. Tryon Palace will be the only site in North Carolina to host the First Oval Office Project during 2026.  It is a “do not miss event” for all ages!

This is a specialty tour and requires a separate ticket.

To facilitate school groups, general admission tickets are scheduled to begin Thursday and Friday at noon and at 10 A.M. on Saturday.

title: Morning Birding Walk
description:
Come along for a Ranger-led morning birding walk at Mayo River State Park. Fresh into the spring season, enjoy the tranquil morning songs of our feathered friends while walking along the unmarked Mayo Meadows trail. This trail is an easy hike through old tobacco fields and is about one mile out-and-back length.

Meeting location is at the Mayo River Bridge Kayak Launch (3100-3110 US-220 BUS, Stoneville, NC 27048) parking lot. Please bring outdoor shoes, binoculars, camera, and a flash light.

If you have further questions, please call the park office at (336) 916-6080.

Please note: This event is subject to cancellation due to weather.

title: 2026 NC Water Resources Research Institute Annual Conference
description: The North Carolina Water Resources Research Institute's annual conference brings together all sectors and all disciplines working in water resources across North Carolina. Here you’ll find water utilities, students, consultants, academics, nonprofits, state agency staff, among many others, working across fields, including stream restoration, water supply planning, stormwater management, water quality and groundwater dynamics, community engagement and many more.

title: Apex Intelligence: Leveraging AI for Small Business
description: AI isn't the future, it's happening now! Small businesses have a unique advantage: we're agile, we understand our customers deeply, and we can implement and iterate without corporate red tape. But tools alone aren't enough. Success requires knowing which AI solutions actually solve your problems, how to use them strategically, and how to stay human in an increasingly automated world. Whether you're running a solo venture, managing a growing team, or navigating the rapidly changing landscape of small business, come join this community of forward-thinking owners ready to harness AI strategically. Together, we'll share practical AI tools and strategies, learn from each other's experiments and successes, and build a supportive network that helps us all stay competitive in an AI-driven economy. Springhill Suites Apex 1100 Marco Drive Apex, NC 27502

title: Bald Eagle Search Paddle with Two Sisters Adventure Company
description:
Join Two Sisters Adventure Company for a Bald Eagle Search Paddle at Jordan Lake State Recreation Area, hosted on select dates throughout 2026. Jordan Lake is one of the best Bald Eagle viewing locations in the region. You will paddle past multiple nesting areas in search of our majestic national bird. While sightings are not guaranteed, Bald Eagles are most common in the areas they visit. Your guide will help you identify and answer questions about Bald Eagles and other birds in the area. All participants should have basic kayaking skills, due to the duration on the water this trip is considered intermediate. Pre-registration is required. Participants will launch from the Farrington Point Access. The trip will last approximately 4 hours. Trips will take place on the following dates:

Thu, Mar 26, 9am-1pm.
Sun, Apr 26, 8:30am-12:30pm.
Sat, May 9, 8am-12pm.
Sat, May 30, 8am-12pm.
Sun, Jun 7, 8am-12pm.

Two Sisters works with all guests to make trips as accessible as possible. Please contact them with any specific accessibility requests so they can ensure they have all the necessary equipment and staff. Free parking is available at the Farrington Point Canoe Launch. No pets are allowed. The cost is $60 per person, and pre-registration is required. Single-person kayaks are provided. Participants ages 15 and older are welcome.

This event is part of our Chatham Celebrates America 250 initiative. See more America 250 events. See more Chatham Sports Alliance events.

Two Sisters acknowledges that the land on which this camp takes place is the ancestral land of the Skaruhreh/Tuscarora, Lumbee, Mánu: Yį Įsuwą (Catawba), Occaneechi, Shakori, and Cheraw Nations. As a part of their mission, they honor and respect the history and significance this land holds for these Nations’ past, present, and future. To find out more about the Native People and Lands where you live and recreate, visit this map.

This session is an opportunity to meet program representatives, ask questions and determine if the MEM program aligns with your academic and professional goals.

title: Agroecology Club Plant Sale
description:
Come by the Agroecology Club's plant sale to get a jumpstart on spring planting. The sale will feature plants primarily grown at the Agroecology Education Farm, a wide selection of herbs and vegetables and house plants. More informationabout the sale will be available on the Agroecology Club's Link Tree and the Agroecology Education Farm website soon.

Proceeds from the plant sale will support the farm's mission to provide a diverse array of experiential learning opportunities for NC State and the broader community, focused on agroecology and sustainable food systems.

title: Legends & Landmarks: Paintings and Drawings by James C. Horton
description: In commemoration of America's 250th, the Bellamy Mansion Museum is hosting an art exhibition by local artist James C. Horton, which includes over 40 original paintings and drawings that highlight historic scenes and places from around the region and country that help tell America's story. Visitors can view the exhibit free of charge any time the museum is open (M-Sun. 10 am-4 pm). Please stop by the Carriage ​House Visitor Center to gain admission. Art is available for purchase and an Artist's Reception will be held on February 27th (6-8pm). Visit bellamymansion.org/exhibits for more information or email info@bellamymansion.org if you have questions.

title: Staying Alive
location: North Carolina Museum of Natural Sciences
description:
March 14 – August 23, 2026

In the ultimate battle for survival, every animal has a plan.

Explore the striking ways nature fights back in “Staying Alive,” featuring 17 live animals from around the world with unbelievable self-defense strategies. Come face to face with trippy toads, armored armadillos, a two-toed sloth and more.

Discover how these defenders deploy an amazing arsenal of high-fashion camouflage and mimicry, specialized armor and advanced chemical warfare – like stink bombs, caboose cannons and smoke screens – to hide in plain sight, stand their ground or even drop a limb and make a run for it. Enjoy live animal encounters, larger-than-life sculptures, 3D tactile experiences and interactive programs.

Perfect for families and animal enthusiasts of all ages. Offered in English & Spanish.

title: The First Oval Office
location: Tryon Palace Historic Sites and Gardens
description:
Experience George Washington’s Mobile Headquarters during the American Revolution!

The Museum of the American Revolution’s First Oval Office Project is coming to Tryon Palace and will be open for tours from March 26, through 28.

The First Oval Office Project is an inspiring handsewn, reproduction of General George Washington’s mobile headquarters that travelled with him throughout the American Revolution.  The reproduction encampment includes the sleeping and office marquee, dining marquee, baggage tent, camp bed, furniture, and more that were part of his mobile headquarters while commanding the Continental Army, as well as other tents for common soldiers in the Continental Army.  A team of interpreters from the Museum of the American Revolution will be on site to guide you and answer questions about his military life while on campaign. Tryon Palace will be the only site in North Carolina to host the First Oval Office Project during 2026.  It is a “do not miss event” for all ages!

title: Wilmington Ladies Tea Walk 251st Commemoration
description:
The Burgwin-Wright House, the oldest and largest historic site in Wilmington, is partnering with the Wilmington Ladies Tea Walk chapter to commemorate the 251st anniversary of the Wilmington Tea Party. Never heard of it? That's OK! The lesser known but still influential moment in history was one of the last local acts of resistance against the Crown in the Cape Fear prior to the start of the American Revolution in the spring of 1775.

At the event, guests will be able to explore the Burgwin-Wright House Gardens, drink tea, hear brief words from a few local historic experts and organizations, and then join in a procession to the riverfront where tea will be burned in solidarity with this act of revolution at the eve of war. Registration is REQUIRED. Please call 910-762-0570 to register or with any questions!

title: Women at the Edge of War: Carolina Piedmont's Heroines of the American Revolution
description:
KMHM is excited to present Women at the Edge of War: Carolina Piedmont's Heroines of the American Revolution. This exhibit highlights the daily courage and civic work of women in the Carolinas, whose efforts were essential to Revolutionary resistance and survival on the southern frontier.

The exhibit will run from Feb. 17, 2026 to May 30, 2026. The museum is open Tues-Sat, 10-4. Admission is free. Check out kingsmountainmuseum.org or call 704-739-1019 for more information.

title: Research Up Close: Plant Sciences Tour
description:
Take a closer look at the research of the N.C. Plant Sciences Initiative, a blooming innovation hub alchemizing cutting-edge technology with one of the oldest and most important fields of study in human history: agriculture and plant science. You'll get to explore the beautiful building that is (green) housing the Plant Sciences Initiative's work and meet a robot whose love of plant photography may help farmers across the globe.

This educational experience is a fun peek into some of the most hands-on and relevant research experiences on campus. Learn how data that lives deep in the weeds can be fruitful not just for scientists but for the food that ends up on your plate and how researchers are attempting to revolutionize the way we understand and care for the plants around us.

Slots are limited, so please register to attend the tour.

title: Cognitive Interviewing in Survey Research
description:
Cognitive interviewing in survey research is a tool that allows researchers to improve survey questions and questionnaires by better understanding how respondents understand and respond to the questions they are being asked. Data gathered through cognitive interviews help researchers identify and remedy issues with their questionnaires. This short course provides participants with
fundamentals on where cognitive interviews fit in the survey process and on how to design, conduct, and analyze cognitive interviews. Participants will have the opportunity to practice specific cognitive interviewing techniques, including think-alouds, probing, and observation. Participants will also learn about revising survey questions based on interpreting cognitive interview results and about the research on which cognitive interview practices are based.

Course Goals:
By the end of the course, participants will be able to:
1. Understand what cognitive interviews are and their use in survey research
2. Be able to prepare for and conduct cognitive interviews
3. Improve survey questionnaires and survey items based on cognitive interview data

Registration and fee (refundable for students) are required for Zoom link access.

title: Hybrid Lunchbox Talk: The Secret Life of Ferns
description:
With Carl Rothfels, Director, Intermountain Herbarium

Thursday, March 26, 2026

12:00 PM-1:00 PM ET

Hybrid - Join us in person or on Zoom!

Free; preregistration required. Register >

What is a fern, anyway? In this talk, botanist Carl Rothfels will demystify (and maybe re-mystify) the fascinating world of ferns. He'll explore their unusual hidden biology, provide tips for fern identification, and introduce some of North Carolina's outstanding fern diversity.

ABOUT THE SPEAKER

Carl Rothfels was born and raised in southern Ontario and was a nature nerd from a young age. It wasn't until his PhD at Duke University, however, that he caught the fern flu. Now a full-fledged pteridologist, he is the Director of the Intermountain Herbarium in Logan, Utah, where he gets to indulge his interest in botanical diversity and particularly his fascination with the infamously confusing fragile ferns (Cystopteris).

title: March 26 - Advances in AI at Northeastern University
description:
Raleigh AI, Machine Learning and Computer Vision Meetup
Join us to hear about the latest advances in AI at Northeastern University!

**Date, Time and Location**

March 26, 2026
9 - 11 AM Pacific
Online. **[Register for the Zoom!](https://voxel51.com/events/advances-in-ai-at-northeastern-university-march-26-2026)**

**Scalable and Efficient Deep Learning: From Understanding to Generation**

In an era where model complexity and deployment constraints increasingly collide, achieving both scalability and efficiency in deep learning has become essential. Scalable and efficient deep learning ensures that powerful models can be trained, deployed, and adapted under limited computational and data resources, enabling broader accessibility and practical application. From understanding to generation, this talk unifies methods that cut costs while preserving capability.

*About the Speaker*

[Yitian Zhang](https://www.linkedin.com/in/yitian-zhang-b54642201/) is a fifth-year PhD student at Northeastern University, advised by Prof. Yun Raymond Fu. His research interests center around Efficient and Scalable AI, spanning Generative Models, Multimodal Large Language Models, and Foundation Models.

**Grounding Visual AI Models in Real-World Physics**

Generative video models have made rapid progress in visual realism, yet they frequently violate basic physical laws, producing implausible motion and incorrect cause-effect relationships. This talk presents MoReGen, a physics-grounded, agentic text-to-video generation framework that integrates Newtonian physics directly into the generation process via executable physics-engine code.

By coupling vision–language models with trajectory-based physical evaluation and iterative feedback, MoReGen produces videos that are both visually coherent and physically consistent. We further introduce MoRe Metrics and MoReSet, a benchmark and dataset designed to evaluate physics fidelity beyond appearance-based metrics such as FID and FVD. Together, this work demonstrates a path toward visual AI systems that reason about motion, interaction, and causality in the real world rather than hallucinating them.

*About the Speakers*

[Professor Sarah Ostadabbas](https://www.linkedin.com/in/sarah-ostadabbas-02606b37/) is an Associate Professor of Electrical and Computer Engineering at Northeastern University, where she directs the Augmented Cognition Lab (ACLab) and serves as Director of Women in Engineering. Her research focuses on computer vision and machine learning, with an emphasis on motion-centric representation learning, small-data AI, and applications in healthcare, defense, and behavior understanding under privacy and data constraints. She has authored over 130 peer-reviewed publications and received numerous honors, including the NSF CAREER Award, Sony Faculty Innovation Award, and the Cade Prize for Inventivity, along with multiple industry and federal research awards.

[Xiangyu Bai](https://www.linkedin.com/in/xiangyu-bai/) is a third-year PhD student in the ACLab and leads the lab's work on physics-aware visual intelligence, with several publications in top-tier computer vision and robotics conferences.

**WorldFormer: Diffusion Transformer World Models with Mixture-of-Experts for Embodied Physical Intelligence**

World models have emerged as a foundational paradigm for enabling agents to simulate, predict, and reason about complex environments. Recent advances driven by diffusion transformer (DiT) architectures have dramatically expanded the fidelity, scalability, and physical plausibility of learned world models. In this work, we present a world model framework built upon the diffusion transformer paradigm, following the design philosophy of state-of-the-art systems such as NVIDIA Cosmos. Our approach comprises three core components: (1) a spatiotemporal variational autoencoder (VAE) that compresses high-resolution video into a compact continuous latent space with strong temporal causality, enabling efficient encoding and decoding of long-horizon video sequences; (2) a transformer-based diffusion backbone that operates on 3D-patchified latent tokens, leveraging self-attention and cross-attention with text embeddings to iteratively denoise Gaussian noise into physically coherent future video states using a flow matching objective; and (3) a scalable pre-training and post-training pipeline that first trains a generalist world foundation model on large-scale, diverse video data and then specializes it to target physical AI domains — such as robotic manipulation, autonomous driving, or embodied navigation — through task-specific fine-tuning.

Our model supports both text-to-world and video-to-world generation, enabling action-conditioned future state prediction for downstream planning and policy learning. We discuss implications for synthetic data generation, sim-to-real transfer, and the integration of world models into vision-language-action (VLA) pipelines for physical AI.

*About the Speaker*

[Yanzhi Wang](https://www.linkedin.com/in/yanzhi-wang-9677a562/) joined the Electrical & Computer Engineering department in August 2018 as an Assistant Professor. He earned his PhD at University of Southern California. His research interests include energy-efficient and high-performance implementations of deep learning and artificial intelligence systems; neuromorphic computing and non-von Neumann computing paradigms; cyber-security in deep learning systems; emerging deep learning algorithms/systems such as Bayesian neural networks, generative adversarial networks (GANs) and deep reinforcement learning.

**Physical AI Research (PAIR) Center: Foundational Pairing of Digital Intelligence & Physical World Deployment at Northeastern University and Beyond**

The Physical AI Research (PAIR) initiative advances the next frontier of artificial intelligence: enabling systems that can perceive, reason, and act reliably in the physical world. By uniting expertise across engineering, computer science, health sciences, and the social sciences, PAIR develops safe, transparent, and human-aligned AI that bridges digital models with real-world dynamics. The initiative is organized around three intellectual pillars: Learning and Modeling the World, through physics-informed multimodal learning, realistic simulations, and digital twins; Reasoning in the World, by integrating multimodal evidence to support grounded decision-making under uncertainty; and Acting in the World, by ensuring AI systems are verifiable, explainable, energy-efficient, and trustworthy. Together, these efforts position Physical AI as a foundational science driving innovation in health, sustainability, and security.

*About the Speaker*

[Edmund Yeh](https://www.linkedin.com/in/edmund-yeh-5a3a976/) is the Department Chair of Electrical and Computer Engineering at Northeastern University.

title: RAPP Session: NIH Multi-component Applications with Foreign Partners
description:
At this meeting, we will highlight recent policy and procedure changes and will share guidance and best practices for submitting multi-component applications with foreign partners to NIH. Given recent updates to this process, members of OSP will walk us through UNC's updated guidance, and we will hear from research administrators with previous experience submitting these kinds of applications.

The Research Administrators Peer-to-Peer (RAPP) group seeks to create a community of practice for research administrators and other professionals involved in research administration across the University of North Carolina at Chapel Hill. The group will participate in meetings and other activities to inform, educate, and support individuals by providing a forum for discussion and an open exchange of ideas; an information network for policies, procedures, and sponsor guidelines; and networking opportunities for research administration professionals across campus.

title: 2026 Writers Discussion Series: Jaha Nailah Avery (UNC Law ’13), Lawyer, author and reporter
location: The Sonja Haynes Stone Center for Research in Black Culture and History
description:
2026 Writers Discussion Series
Jaha Nailah Avery (UNC Law ’13), Lawyer, author and reporter
March 26 | 3:30 PM | Stone Center

Jaha Nailah Avery is an African American woman and proud Southerner, whose family has roots in North Carolina stretching back over 300 years. Hailing from Asheville, North Carolina, she received her law degree from The University of North Carolina at Chapel Hill, where she studied constitutional and civil rights law. She spent several years in the startup tech space before embarking on her professional writing career, and her work can be found in The New York Times, Rolling Stone, Vanity Fair and Architectural Digest. Raised primarily by her grandparents, Jaha has always had a strong sense of identity and heritage and has a deep appreciation for history and oral tradition. An avid traveler, she has traveled across Africa, Europe and Asia. She is a beginner harpist and is currently trying her hand at composing. She is a member of Delta Sigma Theta Sorority, Inc. and a Diamond Life member of the NAACP. Her aim is to always document, celebrate and preserve the stories of Black people, communities, and history.

title: Basket to Table
description:

Step outside, slow down and reconnect with nature. title: ERA - Eno Climate Champions 4.0: Prairie Plantings
location: Confluence Natural Area, 4214 Highland Farm Rd, Hillsborough, NC 27278, USA
description:

Our annual TEEN Eno Climate Champions program is back with a new twist! Local teens are invited to join the Eno River Association for a FREE four-session series focusing on the role of Piedmont prairie restoration in a changing climate. During these sessions, students will learn about the history and current status of Piedmont prairies, the benefits of these ecosystems, and how they can be reintroduced in the area.
In this session, participants will spend the first portion observing and learning about the Piedmont Prairie planting process with our Stewardship Manager. We will then work together to install an informational kiosk that participants will fill with their own Piedmont Prairie informational displays in the final session in April (date TBD).
Recommended ages 12-17 years old. Snacks provided!
