title: Exhibition Opening Celebration
description:
Join us at the Gregg Museum for the opening reception of the current exhibitions this spring, featuring a Native American group show of artists, Native stories and voices.  This special evening is your opportunity to meet the various exhibited artists, mingle with the community, and enjoy complimentary hors d'oeuvres. This isn’t just an opening—it’s a celebration featuring a unique program of live performances, ceremonies, and opening remarks. Learn more about the exhibitions Stories Told by Breath: Native American Voices in North Carolina, A Creek in Carolina: artwork by Bobby C. Martin, and Through Our Eyes, In Our Hands: Prioritizing Indigenous Knowledge in Museums.

5:45-6:15 p.m. | Blessing Ceremony Song by Sandon Jacobs (Waccamaw-Siouan) and Opening remarks from the Gregg Museum Director, Sara Segerlin, collaborators including Nancy Chavis (Lumbee) and Justin Richardson (Haliwa-Saponi), and exhibition artists including Bobby C. Martin (Muscogee Creek) and Senora Lynch (Haliwa-Saponi) 6:45-7:00 p.m. | Performance by the NC State’s Native student group, Miskwa Waya7:00 p.m. | Song in the Gallery with Alexis Raeana (Lumbee)

A special thanks to our collaborators. The exhibitions and public programming were developed in partnership with Nancy Chavis, Curatorial and Education Consultant, NC State Ph.D. alumna, and Director of the Museum of the Southeastern American Indian at UNC Pembroke; the NC State American Indian Advisory Council; and the Native American Student Association. Together, they bring museum expertise, community insight, and Indigenous knowledge to the heart of the 2026 exhibition season.

We look forward to welcoming all at the museum for an exciting evening!

title: Hanes Visiting Artist Lecture Series: Fidenco Fifield-Perez
description:
Department of Art and Art History

Fidencio Fifield-Perez was born in Oaxaca, Mexico, but raised in the U.S. after his family migrated. His current work examines borders, edges, and the people who must traverse them. In his work, Fifield-Perez’s interdisciplinary practice centers the materiality of paper ephemera, everyday self- documents discarded after having fulfilled their purpose. For Fifield-Perez, printmaking, collage, and painting are ways to visualize and connect mental landscapes of past and present.

Fifield-Perez received his BFA from Memphis College of Art and an MA & MFA from The University of Iowa. He has exhibited at multiple institutions, including The Cleveland Museum of Art, the Herbert F. Johnson Museum of Art, Marianna Kistler Beach Museum of Art, the Museum of Contemporary Photography, Chicago, and International Print Center New York. He has completed artist residencies at The Studios at MASS MoCA, Ox-Bow, ACRE, Crosstown Arts, and the Galveston Artist Residency, among others. He has been awarded the Eliza Moore Fellowship at Oak Spring Garden Foundation, 2024 McKnight Printmaking Fellowship, the inaugural Dr. Harold R. Adams Artist-in-Residence Fellowship at The University of Minnesota and is currently Assistant Professor of Painting at the University of California-Davis.

An endowment established in 1983 through the generosity of Nancy and Robin Hanes supports the Art Department's Visiting Artist Series. This important program brings both established and emerging artists to campus to discuss their work in public lectures and to offer individual critiques to our M.F.A. students.  The Hanes Visiting Artist series greatly enriches both our academic programs and our outreach to the wider community.  All lectures are free and open to the public.

A weeknight or daytime permit is now required after 5:00 pm on weekdays. No permit is required from 5:00 pm Friday through 7:30 am Monday. A $1.00 one-night pass is available in selected lots. More information can be found HERE.

Artist website: https://www.fidenciofperez.com/

Contact: Martín Wannam, mwanna@unc.edu

title: The Cuala Press and the Yeats Sisters: Film Screening and Exhibit
description: Celebrate Women’s History Month by learning about the history of the Cuala Press, a small fine art press founded and led by Elizabeth and Lily Yeats, sisters of poet William Butler Yeats, in Ireland at the turn of the 20th century. The Yeats sisters hired Irish women to create hand-printed books, publishing the works of emerging Irish authors and artists during a period of cultural revival and political change. View the documentary film and explore original materials from the Cuala Press in Special Collections.

title: Andrews Launch Accelerator: Launch Sessions
description:
The Andrews Launch Accelerator's Launch Sessions are designed to help ventures articulate, validate and communicate their value to key programs and stakeholders. The schedule for the Launch Sessions is below. All sessions will be held virtually.

Session 1 (Feb. 26): Join us for an introduction to the Andrews Launch Accelerator, the Launch Sessions and the first core principle: customer discovery.

Session 2 ( March 5): This session will explore the often-overlooked fundamentals of customer discovery.  It will focus heavily on understanding your customers' frustrations, dissatisfactions and unfulfilled desires. The session will also delve into collecting evidence of these things through unbiased discovery work.

Session 3 (March 12): You'll learn about the methods entrepreneurs use to rapidly validate demand for what they are working on without spending a lot of money and time.

Session 4 (March 26): With just a few days left before the Andrews Launch Accelerator applications are due on March 31, this session will be an open-floor Q&A on anything related to the Andrews Launch Accelerator, including applications.

title: "Wheel Ruts to Iron Rails" - A250 Presentation
description:
America 250 Semiquincentennial Programs

Explore North Carolina’s early transportation story through a three-part presentation series held in conjunction with America 250 Semiquincentennial events taking place throughout 2026. Scheduled for March, April, and May, the series will highlight railroad development and construction prior to 1860, the origins and use of the Nissen wagon, originally produced in Winston-Salem, and how supplies were moved during the Revolutionary War.

Each presentation begins at 7:00 p.m. and will conclude after one hour. This is a free event that does not require museum admission, but all attendees must pre-register for this presentation. A shuttle bus will be in operation before and after the presentation to transport attendees from parking lot to designated building.

"Wheel Ruts to Iron Rails"

The development of railroads revolutionized the movement of people and goods across the United States and remains a vital chapter in our nation’s story. Discover how early horse-drawn carriages running on metal straps evolved into steam-powered locomotives and iron rails in the late 1820s in England and America. Trace the rapid advancements in locomotive design from the 1830s through the 1850s, as expanding rail lines connected the North and South and opened pathways into the western territories.

This presentation is hosted by Larry Neal, who served several years as a railroad researcher and historian at the North Carolina Transportation Museum. If you need assistance or have any questions before registering, contact Larry Neal at larry.neal@dncr.nc.gov or 704-636-2889.

This presentation will start in the museum’s Roundhouse Orientation Room and include a brief walk through the building.

Restaurants are open on Thursdays, so you can Dash you food right inside, or bring it with you!

Moondog has an extensive and ever-changing collection of games that are usually loaned out for a fee, but on Thursdays (just like on Mondays), they are all free to play. Usually we can find folks to fill in your table or teach you the basics of the games. I've been hosting it for a few months now, and we have had a great time with students returning and bringing in new colleagues, as well as the faithful ones who attended over the summer.

Dogs and outside food both allowed and encouraged. Always free! There is award-winning mead on tap and bottled, as well as an ever-changing collection of beer, cider, THC-infused water, colas and, yes, just plain old water to keep you hydrated on your adventure.

Any questions? Contact us here at Durham Geeks!

title: The First Oval Office Project
description:
Experience George Washington’s Mobile Headquarters during the American Revolution!

The Museum of the American Revolution’s First Oval Office Project is coming to Tryon Palace and will be open for tours from March 26, through 28.

The First Oval Office Project is an inspiring handsewn, reproduction of General George Washington’s mobile headquarters that travelled with him throughout the American Revolution.  The reproduction encampment includes the sleeping and office marquee, dining marquee, baggage tent, camp bed, furniture, and more that were part of his mobile headquarters while commanding the Continental Army, as well as other tents for common soldiers in the Continental Army.  A team of interpreters from the Museum of the American Revolution will be on site to guide you and answer questions about his military life while on campaign. Tryon Palace will be the only site in North Carolina to host the First Oval Office Project during 2026.  It is a “do not miss event” for all ages!

This is a specialty tour and requires a separate ticket.

To facilitate school groups, general admission tickets are scheduled to begin Thursday and Friday at noon and at 10 A.M. on Saturday.

title: Staying Alive
location: North Carolina Museum of Natural Sciences
description:
March 14 – August 23, 2026

In the ultimate battle for survival, every animal has a plan.

Explore the striking ways nature fights back in “Staying Alive,” featuring 17 live animals from around the world with unbelievable self-defense strategies. Come face to face with trippy toads, armored armadillos, a two-toed sloth and more.

Discover how these defenders deploy an amazing arsenal of high-fashion camouflage and mimicry, specialized armor and advanced chemical warfare – like stink bombs, caboose cannons and smoke screens – to hide in plain sight, stand their ground or even drop a limb and make a run for it. Enjoy live animal encounters, larger-than-life sculptures, 3D tactile experiences and interactive programs.

Perfect for families and animal enthusiasts of all ages. Offered in English & Spanish.

title: Innovative AI Communication Workshop II
description:
Join the AI4CommsHub for the Innovative AI Communication Hub Workshop, a two‑part, immersive learning experience designed to help students build practical skills in AI literacy, ethical evaluation and strategic communication.

The second workshop will be March 27 and is best suited for students ready to apply AI to communication practice, industry contexts and engineering domains. Participants will work with advanced tools, analyze case studies and develop communication products that demonstrate clarity, accuracy and ethical decision‑making. Both sessions emphasize hands‑on learning, guided practice and real-world relevance.

Workshop participants will:

Build practical AI and communication skillsParticipate in the AI Creator Challenge and poster presentationsCompete for prizes and valuable packages of online training boot camp coursesEarn recognition as an ethical AI communicatorReceive a certificate of completion upon completing the AI challenge, poster presentation and surveysCreate portfolio-ready projects.Network with peers, faculty and industry-connected mentors.The project is designed to equip students in biological and agricultural engineering technology,  biological and agricultural engineering and other STEM disciplines with essential AI literacy,  ethical communication competencies and hands-on experience using emerging AI tools, such as ChatGPT, Midjourney and domain-specific AI applications in agriculture and engineering. However, this workshop is open to undergraduate and graduate students in STEM, communication, design and related fields. Students interested in AI literacy, ethical technology use, science communication or interdisciplinary collaboration will benefit from the program. Prior technical experience with AI tools is not required.

Registration is required. Early bird registrants will receive a free:

AI learning kit (valued at $50) — includes a custom notebook, a USB drive with AI resources, a portfolio folder, worksheets and collaboration toolsAI training textbook (valued at $50) — a hands-on guide for AI literacy, ethical awareness and real-world application

title: Bonehenge Family Friendly Tour
location: North Carolina Maritime Museum in Beaufort
description:
Children ages 7 to 14 and their companion adults are invited to participate in a new 20 to 30-minute tour of the Bonehenge Whale Center. The free tour is a shortened version of the regular public tour with softer imagery and vocabulary for a younger audience. Along with the opportunity to touch and hold multiple bones, whale oil, and a real whale’s tooth, the tour will include discussions on what whales eat, how they are adapted to deep ocean living, conservation issues regarding whales, and how the public can help.

This tour takes place in an active workshop; therefore, it is not recommended for children under the age of 7. Pre-registration is required by noon the day prior to the program. For more information, call 252-504-7758.

title: Duke Arts Presents: Doric String Quartet
description:
Praised for their "sumptuous sweetness and laser-like clarity" (BBC Music Magazine), Doric String Quartet has earned international acclaim. Tracing a lineage of musical invention, the quartet's program spans the Baroque, Classical, and Romantic eras.

From Bach's monumental exploration of counterpoint in The Art of Fugue, Haydn then evolves those techniques in his String Quartet in F minor, with a fugue based on two different subjects. Beethoven takes this progression further in his Ninth String Quartet, building on the contrapuntal momentum to create a powerful fugue finale.

All three works were written when their composers were at the height of their powers, revealing three masters of the string quartet form. Haydn's Op. 20 quartets, often called the "Sun" quartets, are considered the birth of the modern string quartet, which Beethoven expanded during his intensely emotional middle period. Despite his hearing loss and ill health, Beethoven's creativity flourished, producing some of the most celebrated quartets in the repertoire.

Program
Bach: Four fugues from The Art of the Fugue, BWV 1080
Haydn: Quartet, F minor, Op. 20 #5
Beethoven: Quartet, C Major, Op. 59 #3

title: The First Oval Office Project
description:
Experience George Washington’s Mobile Headquarters during the American Revolution!

The Museum of the American Revolution’s First Oval Office Project is coming to Tryon Palace and will be open for tours from March 26, through 28.

The First Oval Office Project is an inspiring handsewn, reproduction of General George Washington’s mobile headquarters that travelled with him throughout the American Revolution.  The reproduction encampment includes the sleeping and office marquee, dining marquee, baggage tent, camp bed, furniture, and more that were part of his mobile headquarters while commanding the Continental Army, as well as other tents for common soldiers in the Continental Army.  A team of interpreters from the Museum of the American Revolution will be on site to guide you and answer questions about his military life while on campaign. Tryon Palace will be the only site in North Carolina to host the First Oval Office Project during 2026.  It is a “do not miss event” for all ages!

This is a specialty tour and requires a separate ticket.

To facilitate school groups, general admission tickets are scheduled to begin Thursday and Friday at noon and at 10 A.M. on Saturday.

title: NHBA Bird Outing
description:
Contact Rick Wright at birdaz@gmail.com with questions.  The location will be Johnston Mill Nature Preserve, 2713 Mt. Sinai Rd., Chapel Hill, NC 27514.

This outing is sponsored by the New Hope Bird Alliance (formerly New Hope Audubon).

Outings usually last 2-3 hours. No reservations or sign-up needed. If there is a large turnout for any particular outing, we may split into two groups. We do recommend carpooling for families or friend pods; while we love pets, there is a ‘no-pet’ policy for bird outings. New birders and birders of all skill levels are welcome.

Please wear long pants, socks, and sturdy shoes (ticks and chiggers may be present). Sun and bug protection are also recommended. Bring snacks and water if you like. Heavy rain will result in cancellation.

title: Self-Led Scavenger Hunt!

title: Hilltop-Needmore Park (North) Bird Outing
location: Hilltop Needmore Town Park & Preserve, 4621 Shady Greens Dr, Fuquay-Varina, NC 27526, USA
description:
Free Birding trip with Wake Audubon at Hilltop-Needmore Park NORTH
4621 Shady Greens Drive, Fuquay-Varina, NC 27526
Leaders: Larry Zoller and Kyra Thurow Bartow
Location: Lower Clubhouse Parking Lot
Date: Saturday, FEBRUARY 28, 2026, 8:30 to 10:30 AM
Come join Wake Audubon for a winter morning bird walk on the North Trail. The walk is leisurely with frequent stops for birds. There are elevation changes but we often stop to enjoy birds. Please wear shoes good for walking on the dew-covered grassy fairway. Anyone 6 years and up are welcome to join us!

The path we take includes steep inclines, but no stairs.
This walk is usually on the fourth Saturday of most months.

Ebird hotspot site: https://ebird.org/hotspot/L16295752

title: Easter Bunny Express
description:
Throughout our four-day eggstravaganza, event tickets give you access to our museum grounds and exhibit buildings and include a visit with the Easter Bunny! You’ll be able to take your own photos, or for an additional charge, have our professional onsite photographer take your family’s photos. Enjoy the springtime weather as you explore the museum exhibits featuring model train layouts, trains, airplanes, automobiles, trucks, and more.

Upgrade your visit with a leisurely 25-minute train ride experience aboard our specially decorated Easter-themed train! While riding the rails through our 60-acre museum site, you’ll be greeted by the Easter Bunny who will be passing out treats to the kids. Your ticket also allows you to visit the museum grounds and exhibit buildings. Before or after your ride, you’ll be able to enjoy a visit with the Easter Bunny and all the other activities!

title: Marshals Need for No Kings Rally!!!
description:
PLEASE READ!!

Volunteer as a Marshal – No Kings Rally (March 28)

We are recruiting dedicated volunteers to serve as Marshals for the No Kings Rally on March 28th. The march will begin at 10:30 AM, and Marshals are essential to ensuring the event runs safely, smoothly, and successfully.

⚠️ All Marshals must arrive by 10:00 AM (30 minutes early) for training at the march starting location:
📍 211 West Parrish Street

Marshal responsibilities will vary and may include:

-Walking behind the march to help it move safely and efficiently
-Standing at intersections to guide participants along the route
-Helping ensure marchers reach the rally point
-Distributing water to participants
-Monitoring the crowd for any medical emergencies and alerting leadership as needed

Marshals play a critical role in keeping our community safe and organized while we raise our voices together. If you’re ready to step up and help lead from the front (and the back!), we’d love to have you on the team.

Sign up today and help make this rally a success!

https://www.mobilize.us/durhamcountydemocrats/event/907670/

title: Staying Alive
location: North Carolina Museum of Natural Sciences
description:
March 14 – August 23, 2026

In the ultimate battle for survival, every animal has a plan.

Explore the striking ways nature fights back in “Staying Alive,” featuring 17 live animals from around the world with unbelievable self-defense strategies. Come face to face with trippy toads, armored armadillos, a two-toed sloth and more.

Discover how these defenders deploy an amazing arsenal of high-fashion camouflage and mimicry, specialized armor and advanced chemical warfare – like stink bombs, caboose cannons and smoke screens – to hide in plain sight, stand their ground or even drop a limb and make a run for it. Enjoy live animal encounters, larger-than-life sculptures, 3D tactile experiences and interactive programs.

Perfect for families and animal enthusiasts of all ages. Offered in English & Spanish.

description:
KMHM is excited to present Women at the Edge of War: Carolina Piedmont's Heroines of the American Revolution. This exhibit highlights the daily courage and civic work of women in the Carolinas, whose efforts were essential to Revolutionary resistance and survival on the southern frontier.

The exhibit will run from Feb. 17, 2026 to May 30, 2026. The museum is open Tues-Sat, 10-4. Admission is free. Check out kingsmountainmuseum.org or call 704-739-1019 for more information.

title: No Kings, No Billionaires, No Business as Usual
description:
Join a coalition of grassroots organizations (Engaged Defenders for Democracy, Democracy Out Loud! Bull City Indivisible, League of Women Voters) and unions as we say it loud and say it clear, No Kings, No Billionaires, and No Business as Usual allowed here!

We will meet at "The Bull" (CCB Plaza: 211 W. Parrish Street) at 10:30-1. We will march from The Bull to "The Leaf" across from the Farmer's Market. Bring signs that show that we stand in solidarity with our coalition partners as we so no kings in the USA and workers over billionaires.

We have heard many times that people cannot hear the speakers. This time, we are renting a sound system. We ask for donations to please help pay for this sound system. Please go to the GoFundMe page to donate.

https://gofund.me/c03d3bf6a

Please join the Engaged Defenders for Democracy Substack: https://substack.com/profile/115484101-engaged-defenders-4-democracy

For more ED4D Mobilize Events: https://www.mobilize.us/engageddurhamitesfordemocracy/

PARKING:

***Chapel Hill Street Parking Garage 326 E Chapel Hill St, Durham, NC 27701

***Corcoran Street Parking Garage 110 Corcoran St, Durham, NC 27701

***Durham Centre Parking Garage 300 W Morgan St, Durham, NC 27701

BATHROOMS:

***We will have port-a-potties available.

***Durham Central Park under the pavillion 501 Foster St, Durham, NC 27701 (6 min walk)

***Durham County Main Library has bathrooms 300 N Roxboro St, Durham, NC 27701 (8 min walk)

HANDICAP ACCESS:

***Handicap parking extremely limited. Consider being dropped off early.

***No stairs. Just regular sidewalks with normal curb ramps.

***Expect large crowds. No seating. Get there early to find a place on the plaza to be and bring your own chair if necessary.

What began in 2025 as a single day of defiance has become a sustained national resistance to tyranny, spreading from small towns to city centers and across every community determined to defend democracy. Our peaceful movement is bigger than ever.

When our families are under attack and costs are pushing people to the brink, silence is not an option. We will defend ourselves and our communities against this administration’s unjust and cruel acts of violence.

On March 28th, rise up, take to the streets, and say it loud: no thrones, no crowns, no kings. We’re not watching history happen—we’re making it. Join us.

*A core principle behind all No Kings events is a commitment to nonviolent action. We expect all participants to seek to de-escalate any potential confrontation with those who disagree with our values and to act lawfully at these events. Weapons of any kind, including those legally permitted, should not be brought to events.*

title: "Her Heart is Slowly Breaking:" a Women's History Tour of Stagville - 2026
description:
Explore the resistance, resilience, love, and labor of enslaved women on one of the largest plantations in North Carolina.

This March, visit Historic Stagville for a special guided tour of the Bennehan House and grounds featuring the stories of enslaved women from Stagville. This tour highlights the lives of Phoebe Bennehan, Anna Bell, Emma Turner Henderson, and more. Their personal stories, reconstructed with careful research, illuminate the experiences of countless women who endured bondage in North Carolina.

This tour will be offered each Saturday in March, at 11 am, in recognition of Women's History Month.

This tour is intended for adult visitors. It includes content about violence, sexual violence, and family separation. Tours will last about 1 hour. The tour will include walking and standing, both indoors and outdoors, including walking over grass, gravel, and uneven surfaces.

Space is limited. Register now to reserve your space! Tickets are $10 per person plus tax, payable by card when you check-in for the tour on site.

title: Deep Dive Into History: Dyeing for History
location: North Carolina Maritime Museum at Southport
description:
History is most definitely not a black and white subject! From costly indigo from the tropics to humble madder roots from the Mediterranean, explore the dyestuffs that helped fuel global economies and bring color to our lives.

This event is part of the museum’s Deep Dive Into History series, programs designed to give visitors a deeper understanding of our shared past through costumed interpretation. Each Deep Dive delves into a specific theme in Lower Cape Fear and maritime history. Programs run from 11 a.m. to 3 p.m., and visitors can interact one-on-one with the presenter at any time during the program.

title: Hidden Battleship Tour
description:
Hidden Battleship is for the explorer at heart! Join us for an afternoon on a unique, behind-the-scenes tour of unrestored areas on the Battleship. Organized into small groups, the participants are led to areas in the bow, third deck, and below, and the superstructure, including all the way to the very top! This program requires climbing ladders, crossing high hatches, and going through tight/confined spaces. The tour is limited to ages 16 and older.

2026 Dates

► January 24
► February 28
► March 28
► April 18
► November 14

11 am – 5 pm; $75 (plus tax) per participant. This program always sells out, so get your Hidden Battleship ticket now! This program is for ages 16 and older.

title: Specialty Guided Tour: Elizabeth Hemphill's Vanceville
description:
Take a behind the scenes tour of the Vance Birthplace and discover the story of Elizabeth Hemphill!

Elizabeth and her post office became the center of Vanceville, a community that developed in the Reems Creek Valley after the Vances moved to Asheville in the 1840s. Uncover her story and explore our artifact collection on this behind the scenes tour of the Vance house.

Tickets are $10 per person. Register in person or online at Zeffy. Tours are 90 minutes and will take place at 11:00 am and 1:00 pm. If you have any questions, feel free to reach out via email at vance@dncr.nc.gov or by phone at (828) 645-6706!

The event is a time for not only the Native community to come together but also for the NC State community to come learn and engage with this rich culture.

The Powwow and all MSA-sponsored/supported programs and initiatives are open to all students.

title: Signs of Spring: Memory Zines and Phenology
description:
What tells you it is spring? How do other kinds of organisms know it is spring, and what does that mean for them? Join us in looking for signs of spring around Prairie Ridge for this program for adults and learn how you can contribute your own observations to phenology monitoring efforts. Following our walk we will return to the classroom for a zine-making workshop on time, memory, seasons, and the profound changes that take place around us each year and connect us to the more-than-human world.
Come dressed for the weather wearing closed toed shoes and ready for a walk of about a half mile over uneven ground, and bring a notebook and water. Zine-making materials will be provided.

title: MFA 2026 Thesis Exhibitions at Lump Gallery
description:
Department of Art and Art History

Lump Presents: UNC-Chapel Hill MFA Thesis Exhibitions

March 6th - March 29th:

INFRA ULTRA by CJ Murphy / Under the Thumb by Michelle Lisa Polissaint

Opening reception: March 6, 6-9 pm

April 3rd - April 26th:

Nepantla by Wendy Yamilett Moran / Growing Elsewhere: Ghorbatī (غربتی) by Rojano Mohammadzadeh / Blood, Sap, Dirt by Iris Moyan Wang

Opening reception: April 3, 6-9 pm

Lump Gallery is located at 505 S. Blount St., Raleigh, NC 27601

Opening Hours: Saturdays and Sundays, 1-5 pm, and by appointment.

Lump Gallery is pleased to present thesis exhibitions by UNC-Chapel Hill’s 2026 MFA graduating class.

At the conclusion of a rigorous, research-based two-year program, these artists consider the concept of place and their personal relationships to it. Each artist employs a range of media and responds to geographic, constructed, technological, and bodily autonomy. These relationships between place and personal narrative raise broader questions around positionality and society; communication and identity; and human impact. Revelation, purpose, and possibility guide each artist’s research-centered practices.

CJ Murphy creates works that render the intersection of technology, magic, and globalization material by reinvigorating found and discarded electrical waste. Michelle Lisa Polissaint draws on her experience in a strict religious community and deep research into the BITE method to discuss contemporary issues of indoctrination, high-control groups, and authoritarianism. Rojano Mohammadzadeh draws on Islamic prayer, talismanic imagery, text, and ritualized gestures to approach the immigrant experience through what she terms transplantation: survival through negotiating acceptance within a host body. Wendy Yamilett Morán’s practice explores the intersections of culture, realism, and spirituality through painting and ceramics, using their work to examine how identity, memory, and belonging are shaped within in-between spaces. Iris Moyan Wang is an interdisciplinary artist working primarily with ink painting and ceramics to create fluid and erotic hybridizations of bodies and botanicals that reimagine Chinese cultural heritage.

For press inquiries or other questions, please contact uncmfathesis@yahoo.com

title: Duke Arts Presents: John Scofield Trio featuring Vicente Archer and Bill Stewart
description: Over the last four decades, trio format is John Scofield's favorite mode of expression. The John Scofield Trio brings together Scofield on guitar, Vicente Archer on bass, and Bill Stewart on drums. The music is intricate, personal, and complex, highlighting a repertoire of Scofield compositions and jazz standards along with material ranging from Bob Dylan to Miles Davis. The three weave their sound through tight jazz standards and savvy original compositions, playing music that embodies the spirit that has kept jazz vigorous and visceral from the start. "I love to make real jazz-improvising statements in live situations with two of my favorite musicians," Scofield says. "It's challenging. You don't rely on arrangements as much as the intuitive way we play together. You rely on good playing knowing that there's no safety net involved. We're three musicians with a special synergy that unfolds between us. It's impossible to judge your own work completely, but I think the Trio format is always some of my best playing. We hook up as a group and it brings us to satisfying places."

description:
ASA DataFest at NC State, hosted by the Data Science and AI Academy (DSA), is an annual undergraduate data analysis competition where teams unveil a surprise, real-world dataset and present their findings to judges from industry and academia.

The event is fast-paced, collaborative and a great way for students from all majors to apply data science skills beyond the classroom while connecting with mentors, employers and peers across North Carolina.

● Apply analytical skills to real datasets from real organizations
● Build teamwork, communication and data storytelling skills
● Connect with mentors, judges and employers
● Strengthen your resume or graduate school applications
● Join a growing data science community across North Carolina

Visitors can view the exhibit free of charge any time the museum is open (M-Sun. 10 am-4 pm). Please stop by the Carriage ​House Visitor Center to gain admission. Art is available for purchase and an Artist's Reception will be held on February 27th (6-8pm). title: Staying Alive
location: North Carolina Museum of Natural Sciences
description:
March 14 – August 23, 2026

In the ultimate battle for survival, every animal has a plan.

Explore the striking ways nature fights back in “Staying Alive,” featuring 17 live animals from around the world with unbelievable self-defense strategies. Come face to face with trippy toads, armored armadillos, a two-toed sloth and more.

Discover how these defenders deploy an amazing arsenal of high-fashion camouflage and mimicry, specialized armor and advanced chemical warfare – like stink bombs, caboose cannons and smoke screens – to hide in plain sight, stand their ground or even drop a limb and make a run for it. Enjoy live animal encounters, larger-than-life sculptures, 3D tactile experiences and interactive programs.

Perfect for families and animal enthusiasts of all ages. Offered in English & Spanish.

title: Phone Bank with Ground Truth
description:
Link to join: https://www.mobilize.us/join/47683006/1:1d859d5838a314cd00eb29f52a62182ad856e0786f7b2c83e9dedb14ec5b21c5/

Join Swing Left for a Ground Truth phone bank—designed to fix what’s broken in Democratic organizing. We’re launching an innovative new program that will revolutionize how we connect with voters over the phone.

Our goal: to help Democrats rebuild trust, win tough races, and flip the House. We're bringing people together to build the community of real people, having real conversations with real people. During Ground Truth phone banks, we will talk to as many people as possible and focus on listening.

At this phone bank, we’ll:

- Call ALL types of voters -- not just those who vote like us.

- Have real conversations that LISTEN deeply to voters’ concerns.

- Gather insights to share with campaigns, so they can be responsive to voters’ needs and smarter with their messaging, targeting, and outreach.

Don’t worry if it’s your first time—training, materials, and support are all provided. Just come prepared with your phone and laptop or computer!

We recommend using earbuds or using speakerphone to free up your hands as well!

––

About the program:

This isn’t traditional phone banking—it’s a whole new way of talking to voters. Together, we’ll gather authentic insights from communities, use cutting-edge tools to capture what we hear, and share it back with campaigns to strengthen their efforts.

Why we’re starting early: We launched a year ahead of the midterms to build power before campaigns get off the ground.

How we’ll go big: Calling every voter, not just the ones we normally rely on, so every conversation builds a more complete picture of the electorate.

How we’ll go deep: Practicing empathetic listening and real conversations that uncover voters’ “Ground Truth,” not just checking boxes on a script.

Paid for by Swing Left, Swingleft.org, not authorized by any candidate or candidate’s committee.

How to prepare:
Thanks for signing up to join the Ground Truth phone banking program! Together, we can transform how we talk with voters, rebuild trust, and swing the most competitive races.

There will be a training at the beginning of the phone bank, so don't worry if this is your first time.

Please be sure to have your laptop/ computer charged and ready to go!

See you there!

Lump Gallery is pleased to present thesis exhibitions by UNC-Chapel Hill’s 2026 MFA graduating class.

At the conclusion of a rigorous, research-based two-year program, these artists consider the concept of place and their personal relationships to it. Each artist employs a range of media and responds to geographic, constructed, technological, and bodily autonomy. These relationships between place and personal narrative raise broader questions around positionality and society; communication and identity; and human impact. Revelation, purpose, and possibility guide each artist’s research-centered practices.

CJ Murphy creates works that render the intersection of technology, magic, and globalization material by reinvigorating found and discarded electrical waste. Michelle Lisa Polissaint draws on her experience in a strict religious community and deep research into the BITE method to discuss contemporary issues of indoctrination, high-control groups, and authoritarianism. Rojano Mohammadzadeh draws on Islamic prayer, talismanic imagery, text, and ritualized gestures to approach the immigrant experience through what she terms transplantation: survival through negotiating acceptance within a host body. Wendy Yamilett Morán’s practice explores the intersections of culture, realism, and spirituality through painting and ceramics, using their work to examine how identity, memory, and belonging are shaped within in-between spaces. Iris Moyan Wang is an interdisciplinary artist working primarily with ink painting and ceramics to create fluid and erotic hybridizations of bodies and botanicals that reimagine Chinese cultural heritage.

For press inquiries or other questions, please contact uncmfathesis@yahoo.com

title: Apply for the Revolutionary Ideals Webinar Series for Teachers
description:

North Carolina History Day invites you to apply to participate in National History Day's BRAND NEW Revolutionary Ideals webinar series in partnership with the American Historical Association to learn with top historians in the field. Two teachers from North Carolina will be selected to participate in this free opportunity and can earn graduate credits or professional development hours.

Dates: March 30, 2026 to February 17, 2027

Credit: Teachers may earn 90 professional development hours OR three graduate extension credits at no cost.

Deadline and Notification: Apply here by February 27, 2026, to be considered.  All candidates will be notified about acceptance to the program on or before March 13, 2026, and the course will launch on March 30, 2026.

School’s out for spring break? Send your nature lover to a week full of hands-on outdoor discovery celebrating the change of season.
Join us for an adventure through the four elements—earth, water, air, and fire—and discover how each one helps our native plants and animals thrive! Campers will dig into the soil to uncover life underground, explore the magic of water and how it shapes habitats, discover flying seeds, and learn how fire helps forests grow anew. Each day includes hands-on science activities, nature hikes, creative crafts, and storytelling that connects these powerful elements to the living world around us.

title: Communicating Science Interest Kickoff Meeting
description:
Are you a science communicator, interested in the science communication field or want to learn about science communication skills? Do you want to share your expertise in communicating science or share research with new audiences? Are you curious about how the arts can amplify your science research? Are you an artist who works at the intersection of art and science and wants to find science research partners for mutually rewarding arts-integrated research opportunities?

Science communication is a rich field made up of researchers and practitioners who understand that science research needs to be thoughtfully contextualized for increased impact on the public good. Join Arts NC State, the Office of Research and Innovation and the College of Sciences for a meeting at the Gregg Museum of Art & Design to share your expertise or learn about communicating science at NC State. During the meeting, you can propose ideas and topics for a series of Communicating Science workshops that will be held during the 2026-2027 academic year. We’d love for you to participate.

title: High Country Wild Chartering Ceremony (Blowing Rock)
description:
High Country Wild is one of NCWF’s newest Community Wildlife Chapters. The chapter protects, conserves, and restores wildlife and habitat in Ashe and Watauga County. The chapter brings the local community together with opportunities to restore habitat, connect with nature, and learn about important wildlife and habitat topics in the region.
Join us as we get together for a fun evening to celebrate and officially launch the new Chapter. This is an opportunity to learn more about High Country Wild, meet NCWF staff and local chapter leaders, and get involved with conservation at the grassroots level. Big thanks to Blue Ridge Conservancy for letting us use their amazing conservation campus as our venue. We’ll kick off the event with a tour of the campus to search for critters and identify plants.
Drinks will be available alongside plenty of snacks. Bring your ideas, curiosity, and maybe a few random wildlife facts. All are welcome! Space is limited so please sign up now.

title: NC Reads - Daughters of Green Mountain Book Event
description:
Join North Carolina Humanities on March 30, 2026 at 6:15 pm, for its second North Carolina Reads book club discussion of 2026. This discussion will examine themes and topics from the North Carolina Reads book selection “Daughters of Green Mountain Gap.” This discussion features author Teri M. Brown in a moderated conversation. Please note: you do not have to read the featured book(s) to attend a North Carolina Reads book club discussion. North Carolina Reads is North Carolina Humanities’ award-winning, virtual, statewide book club.
From February – June 2026, NC Humanities will host free, virtual, monthly book club discussion events where participants will hear from guest speakers, including book authors and topic experts. Libraries, community groups, and individuals across North Carolina are encouraged to read along with NC Humanities, attend NC Reads book club discussion events, and then host their own local book discussions to further conversation, camaraderie, and community. NC Reads events will be held on the following dates, all events start at 6:15 PM:

February 25, 2026 – Morningside: The 1979 Greensboro Massacre and the Struggle for an American City’s Soul by Aran Shetterly
March 30, 2026 – Daughters of Green Mountain Gap by Teri M. Brown
April 28, 2026 – The Devil’s Done Come Back: New Ghost Tales from North Carolina edited by Ed Southern
May 27, 2026 –The Caretaker by Ron Rash
June 23, 2026 – Night Magic: Adventures Among Glowworms, Moon Gardens, and Other Marvels of the Dark by Leigh Ann Henion

title: The Art and Science of Plant Care
description:
Caring for plants is both an art and a science — part intuition, part know-how. Every gardener, from beginner to expert, faces the same essential questions: When should I prune? How much should I water? What’s the best way to plant, fertilize or repot? In this class, Bryce Lane will bring decades of horticultural expertise to demystify these everyday tasks, showing you not just what to do but why it works.

Over eight weeks, you’ll explore the fundamental practices that keep gardens thriving, learning how to perform each one with confidence and purpose. You’ll uncover the science behind good technique — understanding how soil, water, nutrients and plant biology interact — while also developing the practical judgment that comes only with experience.

Each session will focus on one key aspect of plant care, building a complete toolkit for growing healthier, more resilient plants in any garden setting.

title: Staying Alive
location: North Carolina Museum of Natural Sciences
description:
March 14 – August 23, 2026

In the ultimate battle for survival, every animal has a plan.

Explore the striking ways nature fights back in “Staying Alive,” featuring 17 live animals from around the world with unbelievable self-defense strategies. Come face to face with trippy toads, armored armadillos, a two-toed sloth and more.

Discover how these defenders deploy an amazing arsenal of high-fashion camouflage and mimicry, specialized armor and advanced chemical warfare – like stink bombs, caboose cannons and smoke screens – to hide in plain sight, stand their ground or even drop a limb and make a run for it. Enjoy live animal encounters, larger-than-life sculptures, 3D tactile experiences and interactive programs.

Perfect for families and animal enthusiasts of all ages. Offered in English & Spanish.

title: Plug & Play: Live AI Workflows in Action
description:
Data Science Raleigh
Forget static demos and slide decks. Plug & Play Sessions are all about showing, not telling. In this hands-on webinar series, you'll see tech leaders walk through their AI product in real time, highlighting exactly how it works in the wild: setup, workflows, integrations, all in just 30 minutes.

Built on the momentum of our AI Demo Day format, Plug & Play goes deeper. Presenters slow down and dig in: demonstrating usability, showing off speed to value, and guiding attendees through common real-world scenarios. It’s a rare opportunity to see how tools behave under actual conditions, not just polished slides.

Expect 2–3 hours of back-to-back, engaging walkthroughs. Attendees are encouraged to click along live, ask questions, and walk away with more than awareness—usable knowledge.

**Key Takeaways:**

1️⃣ **Real Product Experience:** See full workflows in motion—not just the highlights.

2️⃣ **Usability in Focus:** Evaluate ease of use, integration paths, and how quickly value is delivered.

3️⃣ **Interactive Learning:** Follow along live and get answers as you go.

4️⃣ **Depth Over Demos:** A deeper understanding of how AI tools actually fit into your stack.

**[Register here](https://hubs.ly/Q042c9tg0)**

title: *VIRTUAL* SF/F Group
description:
Durham Writers Group
**Zoom Info Below Description**

This sub-group focuses on Science Fiction and Fantasy, so if you have anything that falls under that category — be it short story, poem, novel-in-progress or screenplay — then come! We'd love to hear from you.

Our format allows each reader 15 minutes to present their work... followed by approximately 20 minutes of feedback from the other attendees. To ensure a fair opportunity to respond, each attendee is limited to 1 minute of feedback time. (We usually get through three readers.)

If a reader elects to post their work in advance (NOT a requirement!), members will be able to access that work at our Google Group:
https://groups.google.com/d/forum/durham-sff-writers-group

We ask all members to provide a warning, both on the Google Group and during the meeting, if their story features content that some readers might find upsetting. This includes descriptions of sexual violence.

title: NC Science Trails Scavenger Hunt
description: Birds and bicycles take center stage at Lake Norman State Park in Troutman. Located in Iredell County 40 miles north of Charlotte, Lake Norman State Park boasts the region's popular mountain biking trail system, the Itusi Trail, offering nearly 31 miles of single-track trail. The namesake lake is the largest manmade lake within the state, and the park covers about 17 miles of its northern shoreline. Park Lake, set off from the larger Lake Norman by a dam, offers quiet fishing spots and paddling. Heron rookeries on two islands shelter over 25 great blue heron nests. Join us at Lake Norman State Park for a fun Science Festival scavenger hunt during the month of April to learn about science and nature at its finest!

title: Spring Botanizing at Penny's Bend
location: Penny’s Bend Nature Preserve, Old Oxford Rd, Durham, NC, 27712, United States
description:
Penny’s Bend, an 84-acre preserve, has a unique geology and therefore distinct soils that support rare plant species. Join Alec Motten, professor emeritus, and Paul Manos, professor of biology, Duke University, for a relatively easy hike, under two miles. The spring wildflower season is challenging to predict, but we will see plenty! Plan to wear weather-appropriate, comfortable clothes and good walking shoes.
Fee: $28; 20% discount for Duke Gardens members with discount code.
Location: Meet at Penny’s Bend. Driving directions will be included in a program reminder email sent the week before the program. Parking is free and carpooling is encouraged.
Accessibility: This will be a small group program that takes place outdoors and traveling on unpaved trails with some uneven surfaces and moderate inclines.

location: North Carolina Museum of Natural Sciences
description:
March 14 – August 23, 2026

In the ultimate battle for survival, every animal has a plan.

Explore the striking ways nature fights back in “Staying Alive,” featuring 17 live animals from around the world with unbelievable self-defense strategies. Come face to face with trippy toads, armored armadillos, a two-toed sloth and more.

Discover how these defenders deploy an amazing arsenal of high-fashion camouflage and mimicry, specialized armor and advanced chemical warfare – like stink bombs, caboose cannons and smoke screens – to hide in plain sight, stand their ground or even drop a limb and make a run for it. Enjoy live animal encounters, larger-than-life sculptures, 3D tactile experiences and interactive programs.

Perfect for families and animal enthusiasts of all ages. Offered in English & Spanish.

title: Women at the Edge of War: Carolina Piedmont's Heroines of the American Revolution
description:
KMHM is excited to present Women at the Edge of War: Carolina Piedmont's Heroines of the American Revolution. This exhibit highlights the daily courage and civic work of women in the Carolinas, whose efforts were essential to Revolutionary resistance and survival on the southern frontier.

The exhibit will run from Feb. 17, 2026 to May 30, 2026. The museum is open Tues-Sat, 10-4. Admission is free. Check out kingsmountainmuseum.org or call 704-739-1019 for more information.

title: Centennial Campus EV Display and Ride and Drive
description:
Please join the NC Clean Energy Technology Center Clean Transportation team for an electric vehicle display and ride and drive on Wednesday, April 1, from 11 a.m. to 1 p.m. at The Corner, 941 Main Campus Drive.  This free event will educate the general public about the benefits of driving electric vehicles.

The event host encourages and welcomes electric, plug-in hybrid and hybrid-electric vehicle owners to attend the event, educate attendees, display their vehicles and give test drives. Original equipment manufacturers and car dealers are welcome to do the same.

title: Navigating Research Funding Resources
description:
Are you seeking funding for your research and don't know where to start? This workshop covers the basics of researching federal and non-federal funding opportunities using UNC resources such as InfoEdGlobal SPIN Plus. It is ideal for beginning researchers or those who want to diversify their funding sources.

This workshop is offered by the UNC Health Sciences Library and led by Katherine Howell.

title: Meet and Eat With Campus Health Providers
description:
Join Campus Health providers for an informal meet-and-greet featuring free lunch in the LGBTQ Pride Center. You’ll learn about Campus Health services and the personalities behind the care available on campus. The providers will highlight Campus Health services that may be of particular interest to the LGBTQ community. Come ready to ask questions.

The workshop is part of the Pride Center's Well With Pride event series, a celebration of LGBTQ Health Awareness Week, which is March 30-April 2. All students are welcome to attend.

title: Building Brilliance
description:
Join Prevention Services and the College of Sciences for a strengths-based, supportive lunch-and-learn designed to empower neurodivergent students in personal development and college success. The group’s focus is on three core areas: building strong social and interpersonal skills, such as handling conflict, setting boundaries and self-advocacy. The group meets over three sessions, twice each semester. Lunch will be provided. The sessions are below.

Session OneFeb. 18, noon to 1 p.m., Bureau of Mines, Room 201April 1, noon to 1 p.m., Campus Health, Room 2301Session TwoFeb. 25, noon to 1 p.m., Campus Health, Room 2301April 8, noon to 1 p.m., Cox Hall, Room  306Session ThreeMarch 4, noon to 1 p.m., Bureau of Mines, Room 201April 15, noon to 1 p.m., Bureau of Mines, Room 201

title: Centennial SoundBites: Live Music and Lunch
description:
Take a lunch break and soak in the sounds of local artists at Centennial SoundBites, a relaxing lunchtime music series featuring 321 Coffee, local food trucks and some incredible local artists. Whether you come for the tunes, the food, the coffee or just to take a moment and enjoy yourself, we've got you covered.

You'll discover a new performer every Wednesday afternoon with artist coordination by Oak City Music Collective. We'll feature a range of fresh sounds from local solo and duet projects from the Triangle and beyond.

Free parking is available at the Oval West Parking Deck. To park there for free, validate your license plate using our QR code upon arrival.

title: How To Get Started in Research Workshop
description: Are you interested in research opportunities as an undergraduate student? description: The Office of Undergraduate Research can help students create research posters. Join us at one of our research poster workshops to help you get ready to showcase your hard work on and off campus.

title: Game for Growth
description: Come take a break from studying. The Counseling Center's Game for Growth allows students to engage in various activities, such as board games, role-playing games and virtual reality to promote emotional wellness, social skills and personal growth. The activities occur in a supportive environment that encourages self-expression through play, helping students build confidence and develop healthy relationships with peers. All students are welcome to participate as often as they desire.

title: ERA - Wildflower Wednesday - Guided Hike - Confluence Natural Area
location: 4214 Highland Farm Rd, Hillsborough, NC 27278, USA
description:
Registration is required - Tickets drop on March 1st at 8:00am!
Price: $4
*Ticket prices directly support the work of the Eno River Association. Thank you for your support!

Celebrate Earth Month with us for this special Wildflower Wednesday hike series at the Confluence! Join three guided hikes every other Wednesday in April to explore various trails at the Confluence, based on where the best spring blooms are at the time. The Confluence is a magical place for spring wildflowers!

Meet at the pavilion beside the parking lot. This hike will take approximately two hours (sometimes less, sometimes more). Please feel free to bring water and snacks. Wear comfortable, weather-appropriate clothing and shoes. Well-behaved dogs are welcome as long as they are under your control and on a 6-foot leash. Restrooms are available on-site.

The address is 4214 Highland Farm Rd, Hillsborough, NC 27278.
From Hillsborough take Hwy 86 North away from town 1.6 miles from US70. Turn left on Coleman Loop 1.1 miles. Turn left on Highland Farm Rd 2.4 miles. The Confluence is on the left. There is a large sign and a gravel driveway. From northern Orange County take Hwy 86 south after passing Walnut Grove Church Rd take the first right on Kenion Rd 1.7 miles. Turn right on Highland Farm Rd 0.6 miles. The Confluence is on the left. There is a large sign and a gravel driveway.

title: Hybrid Women Working Wednesday Phone Bank

NC Democrats

It's time to find all the warriors for justice!

Building power starts with engaging our neighbors. Join fierce female activists from across the land, Young Democrats, and friendly neighbors on Wednesday evenings from 6-8pm at NCDP Headquarters in Raleigh or on Zoom (link sent in confirmation email after you sign up) to call friendly Democrats to talk about making a plan to vote in the 2026 midterm elections and invite them to upcoming events.

Thank you for stepping up! No experience necessary. You’ll get all the training you need when we meet. If you join us in-person, we have snacks and refreshing beverages. Join us to create a closer community and a bluer North Carolina!

How to prepare:

https://us02web.zoom.us/j/84409850441

NC Dems Organizing Team

title: Team ENOUGH Welcome Session (For Young People Under 26)
description:
Link to join: https://www.mobilize.us/join/46980072/1:a97fcb1f9d2eefa76bde5c456a072080e8a3bb87aaf10d142e007ee52af0d182/

How to join:
Please join our virtual welcome session with this link

Are you a young person between 13 and 25 years old who is interested in taking action around preventing gun violence? Join us for our Team ENOUGH welcome session to learn more about the different ways you can engage.

Team ENOUGH is a youth-led program whose mission is to educate young voices about gun violence and mobilize them to take meaningful action against it. Supported by Brady United, we harness the expertise and experience of today’s youth to advance our intersectional work across the country.

Led by an Executive Council, comprised of students just like us, we believe that diversity is a strength, and that only when everyone has a seat at the table can we build an effective movement to prevent all gun violence in all communities.

For us, preventing gun violence not only requires regulating access to firearms but also addressing how systemic racism and environmental factors cause this form of bloodshed to run especially rampant in communities of color. You cannot talk about gun violence without talking about police brutality, white supremacy, the broken criminal justice system, poverty, homophobia, access to affordable housing, or the often barriers separating disadvantaged populations from physical and mental healthcare.

So if you share this belief, join us for our Team ENOUGH welcome session and become part of the movement to end gun violence in our lifetime.

How to prepare:
We are excited to see you soon!

P.S. This is a call for young people from 13-25 years old who are interested in getting engaged with the Team ENOUGH program. If you are over the age of 25 please sign up for the Brady Welcome Session at https://www.mobilize.us/brady/event/274016/

Event Zoom Link: https://us06web.zoom.us/j/84862510973

https://www.mobilize.us/brady/event/425528/

title: OpenCourts Project - Biweekly Sync
description:
Code with the Carolinas - Triangle
Join volunteers at Code with the Carolinas for the biweekly meeting about OpenCourts, a project to build an open data portal for North and South Carolina legal data.. Starting with aggregate datasets from South Carolina courts, volunteers will be working to adopt this data (and our site being constructed to host it) to standards and measures more usable for accountability and analysis. These principles of analysis are drawn from guidance provided by the legal accountability organization [Measures for Justice](https://measuresforjustice.org/), whose limited work in the Carolinas we are trying to renew and expand.

This effort is building toward multi-state data portal that catalogues and publishes trusted, permissively-licensed court records to ensure open legal data remains a **public good** accessible to everyone. ️

Anyone welcome whether an already identified volunteer on the project or just curious. We do encourage new participants to fill out this [interest form](https://forms.office.com/pages/responsepage.aspx?id=DQSIkWdsW0yxEjajBLZtrQAAAAAAAAAAAAO__cyETRVUOTZDSDlCQ0cxRThFMEVUUFNRWkZES08zSy4u&route=shorturl).

title: Laughter, humor and play to reduce stress
description:
Professional comedians shouldn’t be the only people to use smiling, laughter, humor and play as part of their daily routine. These skills are valuable for everyone. A good smile has long been a key tool for anyone who needs to influence others. Laughter is now shown to improve our pain tolerance. Humor and play can be the building blocks of problem-solving. Together they can improve our happiness and effectiveness.

Workshop Objectives
At the end of this workshop, you will be able to:
Understand the benefits of smiling for:
• Improving your mood
• Keeping a positive focus through an easy exercise
• Building confidence and receptiveness among others
Use laughter to:
• Improve cardiovascular health by practicing voluntary laughter
• Enjoy the social dynamic of group behavior
• Reduce stress
Use humor and play to:
• Have fun by building simple jokes
• Improve creativity by keeping editing separate from the creative process
• Build relationships by saying, “Yes, and…”

title: April 2 - AI, ML and Computer Vision Meetup
description:
Raleigh AI, Machine Learning and Computer Vision Meetup
Join our virtual Meetup to hear talks from experts on cutting-edge topics across AI, ML, and computer vision.

**Date, Time and Location**

Apr 2, 2026
9 - 11 AM Pacific
Online. **[Register for the Zoom!](https://voxel51.com/events/ai-ml-and-computer-vision-meetup-april-2-2026)**

**Async Agents in Production: Failure Modes and Fixes**

As models improve, we are starting to build long-running, asynchronous agents such as deep research agents and browser agents that can execute multi-step workflows autonomously. These systems unlock new use cases, but they fail in ways that short-lived agents do not.

The longer an agent runs, the more early mistakes compound, and the more token usage grows through extended reasoning, retries, and tool calls. Patterns that work for request-response agents often break down, leading to unreliable behaviour and unpredictable costs.

This talk is aimed at use case developers, with secondary relevance for platform engineers. It covers the most common failure modes in async agents and practical design patterns for reducing error compounding and keeping token costs bounded in production.

*About the Speaker*

[Meryem Arik](https://www.linkedin.com/in/meryemarik/) is the co-founder and CEO of Doubleword, where she works on large-scale LLM inference and production AI systems. She studied theoretical physics and philosophy at the University of Oxford. Meryem is a frequent conference speaker, including a TEDx speaker and a four-time highly rated speaker at QCon conferences. She was named to the Forbes 30 Under 30 list for her work in AI infrastructure.

**Visual AI at the Edge: Beyond the Model**

Edge-based visual AI promises low latency, privacy, and real-time decision-making, but many projects struggle to move beyond successful demos. This talk explores what deploying visual AI at the edge really involves, shifting the focus from models to complete, operational systems. We will discuss common pitfalls teams encounter when moving from lab to field. Attendees will leave with a practical mental model for approaching edge vision projects more effectively.

*About the Speaker*

[David Moser](https://www.linkedin.com/in/david-moser-dm/) is an AI/Computer Vision expert and Founding Engineer with a strong track record of building and deploying safety-critical visual AI systems in real-world environments. As Co-Founder of Orella Vision, he is building Visual AI for Autonomy on the Edge - going from data and models to production-grade edge deployments.

**Sanitizing Evaluation Datasets: From Detection to Correction**

We generally accept that gold standard evaluation sets contain label noise, yet we rarely fix them because the engineering friction is too high. This talk presents a workflow to operationalize ground-truth auditing. We will demonstrate how to bridge the gap between algorithmic error detection and manual rectification. Specifically, we will show how to inspect discordant ground truth labels and correct them directly in-situ. The goal is to move to a fully trusted end-to-end evaluation pipeline.

*About the Speaker*

[Nick Lotz](https://www.linkedin.com/in/nicholaslotz) is an engineer on the Voxel51 community team. With a background in open source infrastructure and a passion for developer enablement, Nick focuses on helping teams understand their tools and how to use them to ship faster.

**Building enterprise agentic systems that scale**

Building AI agents that work in demos is easy, building true assistants that make people genuinely productive takes a different set of patterns. This talk shares lessons from a multi-agent system at Cisco used by 2,000+ sellers daily, where we moved past "chat with your data" to encoding business workflows into true agentic systems people actually rely on to get work done.

We'll cover multi-agent orchestration patterns for complex workflows, the personalization and productivity features that drive adoption, and the enterprise foundations that helped us earn user trust at scale. You'll leave with an architecture and set of patterns that have been battle tested at enterprise scale.

*About the Speaker*

[Aman Sardana](https://www.linkedin.com/in/amansardana/) is a Senior Engineering Architect at Cisco, I lead the design and deployment of enterprise AI systems that blend LLMs, data infrastructure, and customer experience to solve high‑stakes, real-world problems at scale. I’m also an open-source contributor and active mentor in the AI community, helping teams move from AI experimentation to reliable agentic applications in production.
source: Meetup: Raleigh AI, ML & Computer Vision

title: Leadership in High Pressure Situations
description:
Under pressure, stress pulls leaders out of their roles and into managing mode, reacting to problems, controlling tasks, and just trying to get through the day. This session explores what happens in the brain during high-pressure moments and why that makes it harder to lead with clarity. We will look at how pressure pushes leaders toward managing instead of leading, and why that matters for your team’s growth and resilience. Most importantly, you will leave with a simple, practical tool to help you slow down, refocus, and lead in a way that aligns with your values, even when the stakes are high.

Workshop Objectives:

• Explain how stress impacts the brain and leadership behavior
• Identify your default leadership lane under pressure
• Shift from managing the crisis to leading through it

title: Fulbright Networking Reception
description:
As part of NC State’s Fulbright Week celebration, we invite you to attend the Fulbright Networking Reception!

This special event will bring together previous and current Fulbright students, faculty and scholars to celebrate the research, teaching and global experiences made possible through the Fulbright Program. It is an opportunity to connect with members of our campus community who have participated in Fulbright exchanges and to learn more about how these transformative experiences advance research, public service and international collaboration.

NC State will celebrate Fulbright Week in honor of the Fulbright Program’s 80th anniversary, recognizing eight decades of advancing research, teaching and public service through global collaboration and life-changing opportunities for students, faculty and scholars. From March 30 through April 3, NC State’s Office of Global Engagement, Office for Faculty Excellence and the University Fellowships Office will host a series of informative and engaging events highlighting the many programs supported through Fulbright.

Fulbright is an academic and cultural exchange program that enables American students, faculty and professionals to study, teach and conduct research in more than 160 partner countries worldwide. Fulbright experiences foster lasting connections, strengthen mutual understanding and support collaborative solutions to global challenges.

We hope you will join us to celebrate NC State’s Fulbright community and explore how you can be part of this global network! This event is sponsored by the Fulbright Association and will include remarks on Fulbright’s mission for global initiatives by Vice Provost for Global Engagement Dr. Amy Conger and Vice Provost of Faculty Excellence Kim Grainger, followed by a slideshow of Fulbright experiences, networking session and hors d’oeuvres.

title: Duke Arts Presents: Bear Grease: The Musical
description: Bear Grease is an unapologetically Indigenous musical and a loving clap back to the classic hit Grease-reimagined through the lens of Indigenous culture, language, song, and humor. Created by husband-and-wife duo LightningCloud and born on Treaty 6 territory in Alberta, this grassroots sensation has become a North American touring phenomenon, performing over 200 shows in community centers, powwow grounds, and major theaters alike. With powwow beats, high-energy choreography, laugh-out-loud storytelling, and an all-Indigenous cast, Bear Grease is equal parts comedy, cultural reclamation, and theatrical celebration. This production radiates a sense of pride and joy that audiences carry with them long after the final bow.

title: Easter Bunny Express
description:
Throughout our four-day eggstravaganza, event tickets give you access to our museum grounds and exhibit buildings and include a visit with the Easter Bunny! You’ll be able to take your own photos, or for an additional charge, have our professional onsite photographer take your family’s photos. Enjoy the springtime weather as you explore the museum exhibits featuring model train layouts, trains, airplanes, automobiles, trucks, and more.

Upgrade your visit with a leisurely 25-minute train ride experience aboard our specially decorated Easter-themed train! While riding the rails through our 60-acre museum site, you’ll be greeted by the Easter Bunny who will be passing out treats to the kids. Your ticket also allows you to visit the museum grounds and exhibit buildings. Before or after your ride, you’ll be able to enjoy a visit with the Easter Bunny and all the other activities!

title: Legends & Landmarks: Paintings and Drawings by James C. Horton
description: In commemoration of America's 250th, the Bellamy Mansion Museum is hosting an art exhibition by local artist James C. Horton, which includes over 40 original paintings and drawings that highlight historic scenes and places from around the region and country that help tell America's story. Visitors can view the exhibit free of charge any time the museum is open (M-Sun. 10 am-4 pm). Please stop by the Carriage ​House Visitor Center to gain admission. Art is available for purchase and an Artist's Reception will be held on February 27th (6-8pm). Visit bellamymansion.org/exhibits for more information or email info@bellamymansion.org if you have questions.

title: Staying Alive
location: North Carolina Museum of Natural Sciences
description:
March 14 – August 23, 2026

In the ultimate battle for survival, every animal has a plan.

Explore the striking ways nature fights back in “Staying Alive,” featuring 17 live animals from around the world with unbelievable self-defense strategies. Come face to face with trippy toads, armored armadillos, a two-toed sloth and more.

Discover how these defenders deploy an amazing arsenal of high-fashion camouflage and mimicry, specialized armor and advanced chemical warfare – like stink bombs, caboose cannons and smoke screens – to hide in plain sight, stand their ground or even drop a limb and make a run for it. Enjoy live animal encounters, larger-than-life sculptures, 3D tactile experiences and interactive programs.

Perfect for families and animal enthusiasts of all ages. Offered in English & Spanish.

title: Women at the Edge of War: Carolina Piedmont's Heroines of the American Revolution
description:
KMHM is excited to present Women at the Edge of War: Carolina Piedmont's Heroines of the American Revolution. This exhibit highlights the daily courage and civic work of women in the Carolinas, whose efforts were essential to Revolutionary resistance and survival on the southern frontier.

The exhibit will run from Feb. 17, 2026 to May 30, 2026. The museum is open Tues-Sat, 10-4. Admission is free. Check out kingsmountainmuseum.org or call 704-739-1019 for more information.

title: Writing a Teaching Philosophy Webinar
description:
How would you describe your teaching philosophy to someone who has never observed your teaching?

Join the Graduate School for a workshop that will offer strategies for distilling the most important elements of your teaching philosophy and expressing it in a clear, succinct narrative. We will especially think about how it can be used to highlight your strengths when applying for academic teaching positions.

title: Local Launch Lab™ helps you master the fundamentals, from defining your audience and goals to building consistent, results-driven visibility.
Concise Communication
description: Do people say that your communication style is too wordy? If so, join the Graduate School for Concise Communication, formerly Concise Writing. This interactive workshop will address the challenge of building clear, concise sentences that represent your science to diverse stakeholders. Using Richard Lanham’s famous “Paramedic Method” and other approaches, participants will leave the session with strategies for user-friendly sentences in reports and other areas of workplace communication.

title: Horseshoe Farm - City birdwalk
location: Horseshoe Farm Nature Preserve, 2900 Horse Shoe Farm Rd, Wake Forest, NC 27587, USA
description:
Early Bird at Horseshoe Farm Nature Preserve (registration required off Meetup)
Saturday, APRIL 4, 2026, 8 am - 9:30 am
Register Off-site only: Requires a login and password:
Login first at https://ncraleighweb.myvscloud.com/webtrac/web/splash.html
Then register for this free event at:

https://ncraleighweb.myvscloud.com/webtrac/web/iteminfo.html?Module=AR&FMID=441387752
Or Click on Search -- Activity Search and type in "EARLY BIRD"
Grab your binoculars and meet us at beautiful Horseshoe Farm Nature Preserve for a bird-watching hike led by Wake Audubon guest naturalist, Royce Marcus (substituting for Erla Beegle) . Spend your morning watching and learning about birds that prefer meadows and forest edges on this approximately 1.5 mile hike. For beginning and advanced birders. Bring your binoculars, or borrow a pair of ours. Pre-registration is required. Limited to City of Raleigh residents.
Meet in the parking lot at Horseshoe Farm Nature Preserve 2900 Horseshoe Farm Rd, Wake Forest, NC, 27587 . Note: Allow time for road construction at 401 and Ligon Mill Road.

title: Paddling at Falls Lake
description:
Come explore the serene waters of the Beaver Dam area of Falls Lake on a guided paddling adventure. This unique reservoir offers a tranquil paddling experience away from motorized boat traffic, making it ideal for beginners and wildlife enthusiasts alike. Paddle the lake and explore small coves while being surrounded by a forested landscape that serves as a rookery for herons, ospreys and bald eagles.

Prior paddling experience isn't required to participate, but participants must be able to swim and will undergo a swim test during a pre-trip meeting. Transportation, equipment and instruction are included in the trip.

title: First Saturday Birding at Brumley
location: Brumley North Nature Preserve, 3620 Old State Highway 10, Chapel Hill, NC, 27514, United States
description:
Come join us for our First Saturday birding hike at our Brumley North Nature Preserve!
Did you know that Brumley North is the #2 Hotspot for birding in the state? Our Brumley Birding hike will be led by co-members of both TLC and the New Hope Bird Alliance. We will meander on an easy 1.5 mile hike over about 2 hours with many stops along the way. Please wear good walking shoes and layers, bring a water bottle, and binoculars if you have them. We suggest that you leave your furry friends at home for this hike as they may scare away wildlife viewing opportunities. This is a free event, but preregistration is required.

If registration is full, please fill out our waitlist form here by April 2nd at noon.

title: ERA - Let's Go Hike: Occoneechee Speedway
location: 320 Elizabeth Brady Rd, Hillsborough, NC 27278, USA
description:
Registration is not required for Let's Go Hike hikes, so come on out and join us at 10am for this 1.3 mile hike. These hikes are a great opportunity to meet new hiking friends, explore the Eno, and learn a bit about this beautiful river.
This hike will take approximately two hours (sometimes less, sometimes more). Please feel free to bring water and snacks. Wear comfortable, weather-appropriate clothing and shoes. Well-behaved dogs are welcome as long as they are under your control and on a 6 foot leash.
Meet your hike leader at the Occoneechee Speedway Trailhead parking lot at 320 Elizabeth Brady Road in Hillsborough.

title: MFA 2026 Thesis Exhibitions at Lump Gallery
description:
Department of Art and Art History

Lump Presents: UNC-Chapel Hill MFA Thesis Exhibitions

March 6th - March 29th:

INFRA ULTRA by CJ Murphy / Under the Thumb by Michelle Lisa Polissaint

Opening reception: March 6, 6-9 pm

April 3rd - April 26th:

Nepantla by Wendy Yamilett Moran / Growing Elsewhere: Ghorbatī (غربتی) by Rojano Mohammadzadeh / Blood, Sap, Dirt by Iris Moyan Wang

Opening reception: April 3, 6-9 pm

Lump Gallery is located at 505 S. Blount St., Raleigh, NC 27601

Opening Hours: Saturdays and Sundays, 1-5 pm, and by appointment.

Lump Gallery is pleased to present thesis exhibitions by UNC-Chapel Hill’s 2026 MFA graduating class.

At the conclusion of a rigorous, research-based two-year program, these artists consider the concept of place and their personal relationships to it. Each artist employs a range of media and responds to geographic, constructed, technological, and bodily autonomy. These relationships between place and personal narrative raise broader questions around positionality and society; communication and identity; and human impact. Revelation, purpose, and possibility guide each artist’s research-centered practices.

CJ Murphy creates works that render the intersection of technology, magic, and globalization material by reinvigorating found and discarded electrical waste. Michelle Lisa Polissaint draws on her experience in a strict religious community and deep research into the BITE method to discuss contemporary issues of indoctrination, high-control groups, and authoritarianism. Rojano Mohammadzadeh draws on Islamic prayer, talismanic imagery, text, and ritualized gestures to approach the immigrant experience through what she terms transplantation: survival through negotiating acceptance within a host body. Wendy Yamilett Morán’s practice explores the intersections of culture, realism, and spirituality through painting and ceramics, using their work to examine how identity, memory, and belonging are shaped within in-between spaces. Iris Moyan Wang is an interdisciplinary artist working primarily with ink painting and ceramics to create fluid and erotic hybridizations of bodies and botanicals that reimagine Chinese cultural heritage.

For press inquiries or other questions, please contact uncmfathesis@yahoo.com
